Background -
Fabrikam, Inc., is a global consumer goods company that is undergoing a digital transformation initiative to migrate its entire infrastructure to the Microsoft cloud. As a key element of this cloud migration, the company will implement Microsoft Dynamics 365 Sales, moving away from the current on-premises proprietary technologies used by its business-to-business (B2B) sales team.
As part of the cloud migration, Fabrikam will adopt an AI-first approach to its business solutions and implement AI solutions, wherever possible, to streamline operations.

Problem Statements -
Fabrikam's infrastructure currently relies on various on-premises systems that require sales executives to use corporate computers with physical keyboards to access business information during customer interactions. Mobile phones cannot be used for these purposes, as the systems depend on keyboard input. As a result, the sales executives spend a lot of time using keyboards to search for data on several disparate systems and file servers, rather than focusing on the customers. This affects the customer experience.
Fabrikam stakeholders are concerned that users will be hesitant to adopt AI. If the AI initiatives are NOT adopted, cost savings will never be realized. Additionally, funding for future AI initiatives will depend on demonstrating an increase in AI adoption month over month. As the AI agent initiative for the sales team will be the first for Fabrikam, the rapid adoption of the agent is a high priority.

Planned Initiatives -

General -
Fabrikam management has prioritized AI-driven projects to improve efficiency, customer engagement, and responsible AI adoption. The current application infrastructure is on-premises and must be migrated to the cloud to support the adoption of these technologies.

Infrastructure Migration -
Fabrikam plans to migrate from its current on-premises infrastructure to a completely cloud-based topology; this will include user authentication, the security framework, and, primarily, the adoption of the services by end users.
All the data from the different systems will be consolidated into a single data source - a common data model that will use a Microsoft Dataverse environment as a single source of truth (SSOT) for the sales team.

Sales Cycle Enablement -
To achieve the company's objectives, Fabrikam intends to implement the following strategies to enhance the sales cycle:
Use low-code development to create a single AI agent that has Dataverse as its core component.
Ensure that sales managers can access unanswered correspondence from prospects and intervene as appropriate.
Replace the previous proprietary software with Dynamics 365 Sales to track sales cycles and customer interactions.
Have the sales executives use Dynamics 365 Sales to track interactions for open opportunities and send follow-up communications to prospects.
Have the sales executives use handsfree headsets to interact with an AI agent when they have questions about internal policies or customer data.

Requirements -

Infrastructure Migration -
Fabrikam has identified the following infrastructure migration requirements:
Azure must be used for all future infrastructure workloads.
The company must follow Microsoft-recommended methodologies for infrastructure migration to the cloud.
Any created AI agents must have their return on investment (ROI) calculated to ensure that the solution will save the company money.

Sales Cycle Enablement -
Fabrikam has identified the following requirements for sales cycle enablement:
The final AI agent must follow Microsoft recommendations for a conversational user experience.
A designated checklist must be reviewed to ensure that the AI agent follows Microsoft deployment recommendations for a compliant solution.
Detailed telemetry must be logged for the first created AI agent to help troubleshoot and optimize the agent during the initial AI agent adoption process.
Unexpected AI agent actions must end in an escalation to a live representative. For example, a sales executive must be rerouted to a representative if the agent cannot answer a question after two failed attempts.
The return on investment (ROI) of switching from the current process to the future process is required for stakeholder sign off.
The sales team must use Dynamics 365 Sales to correspond with prospects more quickly and efficiently than currently.
Sales managers must report on the adoption of the AI agent to key Fabrikam stakeholders on a monthly basis.
Any sensitive information, such as user IDs and names, shared via the AI agent must be tracked for future auditing.
Which framework should you use to meet the AI agent requirements for the sales cycle enablement? To answer, select the appropriate options in the answer area.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.

Which framework should you use for the infrastructure migration?
  • A. Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework for Azure
  • B. Success by Design
  • C. Microsoft Power Platform Center of Excellence (CoE)
  • D. Microsoft Power Platform Project Setup Wizard
data.examAnsDesc:
Why option A – Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework for Azure – is the correct choice

  • The Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) provides a Microsoft‑recommended end‑to‑end methodology for moving on‑premises workloads to Azure, covering assessment, solution design, migration, validation, and operations.
  • It aligns with Fabrikam’s requirement to “follow Microsoft‑recommended methodologies for infrastructure migration to the cloud” and includes guidance on ROI calculation, security, and telemetry, which are explicitly part of the migration objectives.
  • CAF’s migration planning workstreams map directly to the stated needs: consolidating data into a single Dataverse source, establishing a unified authentication and security model, and ensuring audit‑ready telemetry for AI agents.
  • The framework also integrates with other Microsoft services (Azure Migrate, Azure Arc, Power Platform, Dynamics 365) needed for the downstream AI‑agent and sales‑automation components.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • Success by Design (B) – This is a go‑to‑market and consumption model for Power Platform that focuses on licensing and value realization, not a migration methodology. It does not address the low‑level infrastructure lift‑and‑shift or the detailed migration checklist required by Fabrikam.
  • Microsoft Power Platform Center of Excellence (CoE) (C) – A CoE provides governance, best‑practice guidance, and community support for building and scaling low‑code solutions, but it is not a migration framework. It cannot replace the structured, Azure‑centric migration process mandated for moving all workloads to the cloud.
  • Power Platform Project Setup Wizard (D) – This wizard creates a new Power Platform project scaffolding (dataverse, Power Apps, Power Automate templates). It is a development‑level tool for prototyping solutions, not a comprehensive migration framework that covers network, identity, security, and data‑migration phases required for the overall infrastructure transformation.

Conclusion

Only the Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework for Azure supplies the full, Microsoft‑endorsed migration pathway that satisfies Fabrikam’s migration, security, ROI, and telemetry requirements, making it the optimal choice for the infrastructure migration question.

References

  • Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) – Overview and migration guidance: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/cloud-adoption-framework/
  • CAF Migration best practices – Detailed steps for assessing, planning, and executing cloud migrations: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/cloud-adoption-framework/migrate

data.examQue:
A company uses Microsoft Dynamics 365 Sales to manage leads that are stored in a Microsoft Dataverse table named Lead and use non-standard terminology and custom columns.
You need to configure business terms in the Lead table so that Microsoft Copilot controls can summarize the leads efficiently. The solution must minimize administrative effort.
How should you configure the business terms?
  • A. Combine all the fields into one custom field.
  • B. Map the field display names as business terms.
  • C. Add the schema names as business terms.
  • D. Create new business terms for each field.
data.examAnsDesc:
• Business terms in Dynamics 365 must map to standard field names that Copilot can reference – by mapping the display names of the custom columns to business terms, the platform creates readable, searchable labels that Copilot’s summarisation engine recognizes without additional configuration.
  • Option B (Map the field display names as business terms) directly achieves this mapping, requiring only a one‑time entry per term and no changes to the underlying schema. This minimizes admin effort while keeping the terminology aligned with the data model that Copilot consumes.
  • Option A (Combine all fields into one custom field) loses granularity, prevents Copilot from distinguishing different data points, and is not supported for business‑term mapping.
  • Option C (Add the schema names as business terms) introduces technical, internal identifiers that are not user‑friendly; Copilot expects human‑readable terms, so summarisation would be harder to understand and maintain.
  • Option D (Create new business terms for each field) duplicates effort and does not tie the terms to the actual field metadata, leading to unsynchronised terminology and higher maintenance overhead.

Conclusion: Mapping display names (Option B) aligns terminology with Copilot’s expectations, maximises summarisation accuracy, and requires the least administrative overhead.

References

  • Configure business terms in Dynamics 365 Sales: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dynamics365/sales-enterprise/business-terms
  • How Copilot uses business terms for summarisation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dynamics365/sales-enterprise/copilot-summarisation‑terms‑guide

data.examQue:
A company uses Microsoft Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps.
The company plans to use Microsoft Copilot in-app help and guidance to generate responses for internal business processes.
You need to add an additional knowledge source for the business processes. The solution must NOT add new topics to the Copilot agent for the finance and operations apps.
Which knowledge source should you add?
  • A. Microsoft Dataverse
  • B. a public website
  • C. Azure AI Search
  • D. a file upload
data.examAnsDesc:
• File upload is the supported knowledge‑source type for in‑app Copilot guidance in Dynamics 365 Finance & Operations.
  • It can be added directly from the Copilot studio without creating new “topics” in the agent’s navigation model.
  • The uploaded document (e.g., a PDF or Excel guide) is indexed locally and made available to the Copilot engine, enabling it to retrieve context‑relevant passages during user interaction.
  • This approach meets the requirement of adding a knowledge source while not altering the existing topic structure.
  • Why the other options are unsuitable
  • Microsoft Dataverse – Used for structured data storage and custom entity design, not for delivering ad‑hoc guidance content to the Copilot UI. Adding content here would require custom plugin development and would not be consumed as a “knowledge source” by the in‑app guidance engine.
  • A public website – External URLs are not ingestible by the Copilot knowledge‑source service; they would need a separate web‑crawling or Search index, which adds unnecessary complexity and does not satisfy the “no new topics” constraint.
  • Azure AI Search – Although capable of semantic search, it introduces an external indexing pipeline and topic creation steps (e.g., defining index schema, mapping fields). This exceeds the minimal, out‑of‑the‑box solution required for the scenario.

Conclusion – The only option that directly satisfies the requirement of adding a knowledge source without creating new topics is D. a file upload.

References

  • Add a knowledge source to Copilot in Dynamics 365 – https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dynamics365/customer-engagement/copilot/knowledge-source
  • Upload and manage files as knowledge sources – https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dynamics365/customer-engagement/copilot/upload-files

data.examQue:
A company has an AI business solution.
You need to extend the solution so that Microsoft 365 Copilot can invoke external logic hosted in Azure services.
What should you include in the solution?
  • A. Microsoft Copilot Studio skills
  • B. Microsoft Power Platform connectors
  • C. custom engine agents
data.examAnsDesc:
• Correct choice – Microsoft Power Platform connectors
  • Power Platform connectors provide a standardized, bi‑directional integration layer that enables Azure‑hosted logic (e.g., Azure Functions, Azure Logic Apps, Azure API Management) to be exposed as a connector. This allows Microsoft 365 Copilot to call external services directly, leveraging the connector’s authentication, schema mapping, and invocation capabilities. The integration is managed through the Power Platform connector catalog, supports custom OpenAPI definitions, and can be securely shared across tenant boundaries, making it the recommended mechanism for exposing Azure services to Copilot.
  • Why option A (Microsoft Copilot Studio skills) is less suitable
  • Copilot Studio skills are primarily used for building conversational agents within Copilot Studio and are not designed to expose arbitrary Azure APIs to Microsoft 365 Copilot. Skills operate within the confines of the studio environment and lack the necessary connector infrastructure for secure, governed, cross‑service calls. Consequently, they cannot serve as a robust integration point for external Azure logic.
  • Why option C (custom engine agents) is less suitable
  • Custom engine agents refer to bespoke AI engine implementations that run outside the Microsoft 365 ecosystem. They do not provide a native, supported bridge to Microsoft 365 Copilot and generally require custom middleware that must be manually provisioned, monitored, and maintained. This approach bypasses the standardized connector model, resulting in higher operational overhead, limited governance, and potential compliance risks.

References

  • Power Platform connectors documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-platform/connectors/
  • Extend Copilot with custom connectors: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/copilot-extensibility/connectors/

data.examQue:
A financial services company uses Microsoft Dynamics 365 Finance.
Currently, the company's support staff manually reviews customer transaction histories to detect potential fraud cases before escalating the cases.
You need to recommend an automation solution for the review process. The solution must ensure that escalations reach a human analyst for final decision making. What should you recommend?
  • A. Deploy an autonomous agent that closes non-fraud cases automatically.
  • B. Use Microsoft 365 Copilot in Word to automatically finalize fraud detection policies.
  • C. Configure a task agent to generate fraud risk scores for the human analyst to review.
  • D. Export the data to a data lake for analysis in Microsoft Power BI.
data.examAnsDesc:
Why option C is the best choice

  • Task agents in Dynamics 365 Finance can ingest transaction data, apply fraud‑detection models, and output a risk score.
  • The generated score is presented to the human analyst for final review and escalation decision, satisfying the requirement that “escalations reach a human analyst”.
  • This approach leverages built‑in business process automation (e.g., Power Automate/Flow) to pre‑filter records, reducing manual effort while keeping a human in the decision loop.
  • It integrates natively with Dynamics 365 Finance security roles and audit trails, ensuring compliance and traceability.

Why the other options are unsuitable

  • A. Deploy an autonomous agent that closes non‑fraud cases automatically – This removes the human analyst from the escalation path, violating the explicit requirement that a human must perform the final decision.

  • B. Use Microsoft 365 Copilot in Word to automatically finalize fraud detection policies – Copilot is a productivity assistant, not a workflow automation tool for risk scoring; it does not connect to Finance data or enforce human‑in‑the‑loop escalation.

  • D. Export the data to a data lake for analysis in Microsoft Power BI – Moving data to Power BI creates a reporting layer but does not provide an automated escalation mechanism or guarantee that analysts receive the information in a timely, decision‑oriented format within the Finance environment.

References

  • Task agents and AI‑driven business process automation in Dynamics 365 Finance: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dynamics365/finance/audit-management/automated-risk-scoring
  • Human‑in‑the‑loop escalation patterns with Power Automate: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-automate/automated-flows-human-in-the-loop

data.examQue:
A company plans to deploy a Microsoft Copilot Studio agent that will analyze historical business data to predict customer behavior.
The data is currently stored in an Azure SQL database, flat files, APIs, and logs.
You need to organize the data into a format that can be used as a knowledge source in Copilot Studio.
What should you include in the solution?
  • A. Azure AI Search
  • B. Azure Data Lake Storage
  • C. Azure Cosmos DB
  • D. Azure Translator in Foundry Tools
data.examAnsDesc:
Justification

  • Azure AI Search is purpose‑built for ingestion, indexing, and semantic search over heterogeneous data sources—including structured relational tables, files, APIs, and logs—making it the ideal knowledge‑store layer for a Copilot Studio agent that must retrieve contextual business data in real time. Its built‑in support for relational connections, vector indexing, and AI‑enhanced query pipelines enables low‑latency retrieval of historical business data needed for predictive modelling.

  • Azure Data Lake Storage offers massive, retained raw storage but lacks native indexing, full‑text search, or query optimization needed for efficient knowledge‑source retrieval; it would require additional services (e.g., Synapse, Event‑Based Ingest) to make the data searchable.

  • Azure Cosmos DB provides globally replicated NoSQL data with low‑latency reads, yet it is optimized for transactional workloads rather than comprehensive document‑level search and analytics; without integrated search capabilities it would not satisfy the requirement for a unified knowledge source.

  • Azure Translator in Foundry Tools is focused on language translation and offers no data storage, indexing, or retrieval features; therefore it cannot serve as a knowledge‑source repository.

Conclusion – Azure AI Search directly addresses the need for scalable, indexed, and semantically searchable data across multiple storage formats, aligning perfectly with the knowledge‑source requirements of a Copilot Studio agent.

References

  • Azure AI Search documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/
  • Copilot Studio knowledge‑source integration guide: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/copilot-studio/knowledge-sources/​

data.examQue:
A retail company plans to deploy Microsoft Copilot Studio agents to support:
Microsoft Dynamics 365 Commerce scenarios.
A Microsoft Power Apps inventory management solution.
You need to recommend a solution to organize product catalog data as a consistent source for multiple AI systems.
What should you recommend?
  • A. Let each agent scrape product details from Microsoft SharePoint Online libraries.
  • B. Store the product catalog data in a separate custom table for each agent.
  • C. Configure prompts to pull product details from the PDFs of external vendors.
  • D. Centralize the product catalog data in Microsoft Dataverse and expose the data to both agents.
data.examAnsDesc:
Why Option D is the correct recommendation

  • Single source of truth – Centralizing product catalog data in Microsoft Dataverse guarantees that every system consumes the exact same, up‑to‑date records, eliminating synchronization drift between agents and Power Apps.
  • Built‑in governance & security – Dataverse enforces row‑level security, field‑level permissions, and data‑type validation, which are essential for e‑commerce compliance and for protecting proprietary product information.
  • Native connectivity – Both Copilot Studio and Power Apps provide first‑class connectors to Dataverse, allowing the agents to query, update, and cache catalog items without custom APIs or scraping logic.
  • Scalability & performance – Dataverse scales horizontally, supports rich query capabilities (filter, order, lookup), and can be indexed for fast retrieval—critical for handling large retail catalogs.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • A – Scrape from SharePoint

    • Relies on unstructured file content and manual extraction scripts.
    • Introduces latency, potential data loss, and lacks schema enforcement.
    • Increases operational overhead and breaks governance contracts.

  • B – Separate custom tables per agent

    • Creates data duplication, increasing storage cost and risk of inconsistency.
    • Requires duplicated maintenance (schema changes must be applied repeatedly).
    • Introduces synchronization challenges when product information evolves.

  • C – Prompt‑based extraction from vendor PDFs

    • Depends on static PDFs that are often outdated or mismatched in format.
    • Harvesting data via natural‑language prompts is brittle and not deterministic.
    • Limits support for complex catalog attributes (e.g., hierarchical categories, multimedia assets).

Conclusion
Centralizing the catalog in Dataverse delivers a governed, performant, and future‑proof data foundation that both Copilot agents and Power Apps can consume reliably.

References

  • Copilot Studio documentation – Connecting to Dataverse: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-copilot-studio/connectors/dataverse
  • Power Apps & Power Automate – Using Dataverse as a data source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-apps/maker/data-platform/dataverse-intro

These resources provide detailed guidance on configuring agents to read and write data from a unified Dataverse environment.

data.examQue:
A company has a portfolio of AI initiatives at different stages of development.
You need to recommend a structured approach to evaluating the return on AI investment (ROAI) across all the initiatives. The solution must balance immediate results with long-term values and strategic innovations.
What should you include in the recommendation?
  • A. a simple cost and benefit analysis
  • B. a horizon-based framework
  • C. the internal rate of return (IRR) function
  • D. a prioritization grid
data.examAnsDesc:
Justification

  • Horizon‑based framework (Correct option)

    • Aligns initiatives with distinct time‑frames (short‑term, medium‑term, long‑term) to capture both immediate efficiencies and future strategic innovation.
    • Enables weighting of financial returns, risk exposure, and capability development across horizons, facilitating a balanced portfolio view.
    • Supports dynamic re‑evaluation as models mature, ensuring that early‑stage experiments are not over‑prioritized and that high‑impact long‑term investments receive appropriate attention.
    • Provides a repeatable structure that can be documented in governance artefacts (e.g., investment charter, ROI dashboard).

  • Why the other options are less suitable

    • Simple cost‑benefit analysis – focuses only on direct expenses and revenue; it ignores timing, uncertainty, and strategic fit across multi‑year horizons.
    • Internal Rate of Return (IRR) function – useful for single‑project financial modelling but does not address portfolio‑level alignment or the need to balance near‑term results with future innovation.
    • Prioritization grid – helpful for ranking projects but lacks a formal horizon dimension; without explicit time‑frame weighting it can bias selections toward low‑risk, low‑impact initiatives.

Therefore, a horizon‑based framework best satisfies the requirement to “balance immediate results with long‑term values and strategic innovations” across a heterogeneous AI portfolio.

References

  • Microsoft AI Business School – Delivering AI ROI: A strategic guide (2023) – https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/ai-roi-strategy/
  • Microsoft Azure Architecture Center – AI investment planning and ROI framework (2022) – https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/framework/ai-innovation/roi

What should you include in the recommendation?
  • A. Microsoft Dataverse
  • B. Azure Data Lake Storage
  • C. a Microsoft Power BI semantic model
  • D. Azure Cosmos DB
data.examAnsDesc:
Justification

  • Microsoft Dataverse is the native, purpose‑built data platform of the Power Platform. It offers a single, governed repository that can ingest and harmonise data from on‑premises, cloud, and external SaaS sources, making it ideal for consolidating disparate inputs into one spot for AI consumption.
  • It includes built‑in data classification, sensitivity labeling, and security policies (via Azure Information Protection integration), satisfying the requirement for automatic protection of regulated datasets.
  • Dataverse supports direct connectivity from Copilot Studio agents, Dynamics 365 applications, and external AI models through out‑of‑the‑box connectors and the Power Platform’s AI Builder capabilities, enabling it to serve as the grounding layer for analytics and AI‑driven workflows.
  • While still capable of powering analytics, Dataverse maintains strong data‑model fidelity and query performance needed for downstream reporting without sacrificing governance.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • Azure Data Lake Storage provides massive raw storage but lacks native, out‑of‑the‑box classification and protection policies; additional services (e.g., Azure Purview) must be layered on to meet the governance requirement.
  • Power BI semantic model is optimized for visual analytics consumption and does not natively host or expose the data to multiple AI agents or external models; it also offers limited built‑in classification.
  • Azure Cosmos DB delivers a globally distributed NoSQL database with high scalability, yet it requires custom development to implement data‑policy controls and does not integrate directly with Power Platform’s native AI services for unified grounding.

Conclusion: The only solution that simultaneously fulfills data centralisation, built‑in classification/protection, and seamless AI grounding across Copilot Studio, Dynamics 365, and external models is Microsoft Dataverse.

References

  • Microsoft Dataverse documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-platform/data-platform/
  • Data loss prevention & sensitivity labeling in Dataverse: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-platform/admin/sensitivity-labeling-dataverse​

data.examQue:
A company plans to deploy an AI-based customer service app that will autonomously manage interactions, escalate complex cases, and learn from historical ticket data.
You need to perform a return on AI investment (ROAI) analysis of the app deployment. The solution must ensure that the analysis is accurate.
What should you do first?
  • A. Establish the AI performance metrics.
  • B. Conduct an AI market benchmarking study.
  • C. Model the customer experience.
  • D. Identify and quantify all the development, deployment, and operating costs.
data.examAnsDesc:
Why option D is the correct first step

  • ROI (or ROAI) accuracy depends on a complete cost model. Before any performance metrics or market data can be evaluated, you must capture every expense associated with the solution—development, integration, infrastructure, licensing, ongoing maintenance, and training.
  • Quantified costs provide the denominator for any return calculation. Without a baseline financial footprint, estimates of cost‑savings, productivity gains, or revenue uplift cannot be translated into a reliable return figure.
  • All subsequent analyses (metrics, benchmarking, CX modeling) rely on this cost foundation. Having the cost data enables you to align AI performance indicators with business outcomes and to assess whether the projected benefits outweigh the investment.

Why the other options are less suitable as the first action

  • A – Establish the AI performance metrics: Metrics are essential for measuring success, but they only have meaning when you know the financial context in which they will be applied. Selecting metrics prematurely can lead to mis‑aligned KPIs.
  • B – Conduct an AI market benchmarking study: Benchmarks are useful for setting realistic expectations, yet they do not address the organization‑specific investment required. Benchmarking without knowing your own cost structure risks irrelevant comparisons.
  • C – Model the customer experience: While CX modeling informs the value proposition, its quantitative impact must be tied to cost and revenue forecasts derived from a full cost model; otherwise the financial justification remains speculative.

Key takeaway:

Start by identifying and quantifying all development, deployment, and operating costs (Option D). This creates a solid financial baseline that makes every later analysis—metric definition, benchmarking, and CX modeling—accurate, comparable, and defensible in a ROAI assessment.

References

  • Microsoft AI Business Cases & ROI Toolkit – Guide on building ROI models for AI solutions: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/ai-roi
  • ROI Calculator for Azure AI Projects – Interactive calculator to estimate costs and benefits: https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/pricing/calculator/?service=ai-generator

data.examQue:
A company has a Microsoft 365 tenant in Canada and multiple Microsoft Power Platform environments in Canada and the United States.

The company plans to deploy a Microsoft Copilot Studio agent to the Canadian environment that will use:

•Microsoft Dataverse data stored in Canada
•A connector that connects to an Azure OpenAI instance in the United States

You need to ensure that the agent adheres to data residency and data movement policies before being deployed.

What should you do?
  • A. Ensure that the data processed by Azure OpenAI is stored in the United States.
  • B. From the Microsoft Purview portal, validate the Data loss prevention settings.
  • C. Migrate the tenant to the United States.
  • D. Ensure that cross-region data movement is enabled for the Canadian environment and connector dependencies.
data.examAnsDesc:
Correct option: D. Ensure that cross‑region data movement is enabled for the Canadian environment and connector dependencies.

Why this is the best choice

  • The Copilot Studio agent will process data in Canada but must call an Azure OpenAI service located in the United States.
  • Data residency rules require that any cross‑region transfer be governed by the cross‑region data movement settings of the Power Platform environment, which control whether data can be sent outside the region and under what compliance controls.
  • Enabling this feature makes the data movement compliant with the tenant’s data‑residency and DLP policies, ensuring that the agent’s interaction with the US‑based service does not violate Microsoft’s residency commitments.

Why the other options are unsuitable

  • A. Ensure that the data processed by Azure OpenAI is stored in the United States. – Storing the output in the U.S. does not address the movement of data into the U.S.; the governing factor is the movement permission, not just storage location.
  • B. From the Microsoft Purview portal, validate the Data loss prevention settings. – While DLP is important, it does not control the ability to move data across regions; you still need explicit cross‑region data movement configuration.
  • C. Migrate the tenant to the United States. – Relocating the entire tenant is unnecessary and would disrupt existing Canadian‑based resources; the architecture can remain Canada‑centric if cross‑region movement is properly allowed.

Implementation steps (high‑level)

  1. In the Power Platform admin center for the Canadian environment, locate Settings > Resources > Data residency.
  2. Enable Cross‑region data movement for the environment and whitelist the Azure OpenAI region endpoint.
  3. Verify that the connector used by Copilot Studio is configured with the appropriate regional endpoint pointing to the U.S. Azure OpenAI instance.
  4. Validate the configuration with a test run of the agent and confirm that no inadvertent data residency violations are reported by Microsoft Compliance Manager.

References

  • Cross‑region data movement in Power Platform
  • Data residency and compliance for Azure OpenAI Service

data.examQue:
A company has two Microsoft Power Platform environments named Dev1 and Prod1. A Microsoft Copilot Studio agent named Agent1 is built into a solution in the Dev1 environment.

You plan to deploy Agent1 to Prod1.

You need to make Agent1 available to the users in Prod1. The solution must minimize administrative effort.

What should you do?
  • A. Share Agent1 with the users in Prod1.
  • B. Export the solution as an unmanaged solution and import the solution into Prod1.
  • C. Export the solution as a managed solution and import the solution into Prod1.
  • D. Create a new Copilot Studio agent in Prod1 by replicating the configuration of Agent1.
data.examAnsDesc:
Technical Justification

  • Option C – Export the solution as a managed solution and import it into Prod1

    • A managed solution can be imported into another environment only after the target environment has a matching version of the solution’s components. Exporting Agent1 as a managed package preserves all metadata (flows, custom entities, AI models, security roles, and Copilot settings) in an immutable state that is appropriate for production.
    • Importing a managed solution overwrites any duplicate components with the exact version from Dev1, guaranteeing identical behavior in Prod1 while preventing users from accidentally editing the production configuration.
    • The import process is automated (single click or Azure DevOps pipeline), minimizing ongoing admin effort after the initial deployment.

  • Option A – Share Agent1 with the users in Prod1

    • “Sharing” a Copilot Studio agent is only applicable when the agent is already deployed to the target environment. In Dev1 the agent lives inside a solution that has not yet been moved to Prod1, so sharing has no effect here. It does not address the need to move the underlying solution.

  • Option B – Export the solution as an unmanaged solution and import it into Prod1

    • Unmanaged solutions can be edited after import, which violates the principle of a stable production environment. Importing an unmanaged package into Prod1 would allow users to modifyflows or entities directly in production, increasing the risk of configuration drift and requiring continuous admin oversight.

  • Option D – Create a new Copilot Studio agent in Prod1 by replicating the configuration of Agent1

    • Replicating the configuration manually is labor‑intensive and error‑prone. It bypasses the solution export/import mechanism that preserves dependency links (e.g., custom connectors, environment variables) and does not guarantee version parity, leading to higher maintenance overhead.

Conclusion – Exporting Agent1 as a managed solution and importing it into Prod1 provides a repeatable, low‑maintenance migration that preserves all configuration integrity and enforces a controlled, production‑ready deployment.

References

  • Export and import solutions – Power Platform
  • Managed vs. unmanaged solutions – Power Platform
  • Move a solution between environments – Power Apps (applies to Copilot Studio solutions as well)

data.examQue:
A company has a Microsoft Power Platform environment that contains Microsoft Dataverse data.

You create a Microsoft Copilot Studio agent named Agent1 that processes the Dataverse data.

You discover that Agent1 fails to return relevant or accurate results.

You need to improve the quality and reliability of data grounding.

What should you do?
  • A. Retrain Agent1.
  • B. Verify and cleanse the Dataverse data.
  • C. Use an adaptive card in Agent1.
  • D. Add example user inputs to the training data of Agent1.
data.examAnsDesc:
Why option B is correct

  • Data grounding relies on the quality of the source data; if the Dataverse tables contain inconsistencies, missing values, or irrelevant records, the Copilot Studio agent cannot synthesize accurate answers.
  • Cleaning and validating the data (e.g., removing duplicates, correcting schema mismatches, applying proper column types) directly improves the context that the agent uses for retrieval and response generation.
  • This step ensures that any subsequent enhancements (retraining, example inputs, adaptive cards) are built on a trustworthy dataset, leading to more relevant and reliable outputs.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • A – Retrain Agent1 – Retraining modifies the model’s parameters, but the underlying grounding problem stems from bad data; without fixing the source, retraining will not resolve inaccuracies.
  • C – Use an adaptive card – Adaptive cards improve UI rendering but do not affect the underlying information retrieval or semantic relevance of the agent’s answers.
  • D – Add example user inputs – Adding examples can help clarify intent, yet if the grounded data is flawed, the agent still lacks the correct context to produce accurate responses.

Conclusion – The most direct and effective action is to verify and cleanse the Dataverse data (option B) to ensure the grounding foundation is solid before applying any model‑level changes.

References

  • Microsoft Docs – Grounding your Copilot: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cognitiveservices/cus/customization?view=azuresdk-python
  • Microsoft Power Platform – Data policies and cleansing best practices: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-platform/admin/data-policies

D

[place your answer here]

action:

result:

data.examQue:
A manufacturing company wants to deploy an agent that will automate supplier invoice processing.

You are designing a solution to evaluate the financial implications of the deployment. The company is especially concerned about budget overruns.

You need to ensure that the solution considers the total cost of ownership (TCO), the expected savings from using automation, and whether to extend the existing AI capabilities.

What should you include in the design?
  • A. a break-even analysis only
  • B. adopting prebuilt agents to reduce the deployment time
  • C. training a custom model
  • D. a return on AI investment (ROAI) analysis
data.examAnsDesc:
Design element to include:

  • Return on AI Investment (ROAI) analysis – a quantitative evaluation that combines the total cost of ownership (TCO) with the projected financial benefits of the automation (e.g., labor cost avoidance, processing‑time reductions, error‑related savings). The ROAI framework explicitly models both upfront expenditures (hardware, licensing, integration, training, change‑management) and recurring costs, then compares them against quantified savings and revenue‑impact over the solution’s lifecycle. This holistic view enables the company to forecast cash‑flow, identify break‑even points, and safeguard against budget overruns, making it the most complete choice for assessing the financial implications of deploying an invoice‑automation agent.

Why the other options are less appropriate

  • A. Break‑even analysis only – a break‑even study isolates the point at which costs are recovered but ignores indirect financial gains (e.g., improved cash flow, strategic insights) and broader TCO elements such as ongoing maintenance, agent upgrades, and scalability costs.
  • B. Adopting pre‑built agents to reduce deployment time – while this can lower initial labor costs, it does not by itself address whether the investment yields positive long‑term returns or how the solution will scale and evolve.
  • C. Training a custom model – building a bespoke model may offer functional advantages but typically raises R&D, data‑annotation, and model‑maintenance expenses, making it harder to justify financially without a ROI framework that evaluates total savings versus higher upfront costs.

Therefore, incorporating a ROAI analysis aligns with best‑practice financial governance for AI deployments, ensuring the company can objectively weigh the full TCO against expected savings and decide on extending existing AI capabilities.

References

  • Microsoft AI Business Cases – ROI & TCO Guide: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/ai-builder/ai-roi-tco
  • Azure Cognitive Services Documentation – Cost Management: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cognitive-services/cost-management-overview

data.examQue:
A company has a Microsoft Power Platform solution that contains the following components:

•Microsoft Dataverse tables
•A Microsoft Power BI workspace named WS1
•A canvas app named App1 that uses Dataverse
•A Power BI semantic model that connects to Dataverse by using DirectQuery

You plan to use generative AI to provide answers to queries based on a subset of corporate data.

You need to ensure that the data is available as a grounding data source for AI systems.

What should you do?
  • A. Populate a Dataverse table.
  • B. Share WS1.
  • C. Endorse the semantic model.
  • D. Export the semantic model.
data.examAnsDesc:
Technical justification

  • The Power BI semantic model already encapsulates the curated Dataverse schema, relationships, and business‑logic calculations required for the grounding data set.
  • Endorsing the semantic model registers it with the Power BI Service’s AI‑ready catalog, exposing its metadata to AI services such as Microsoft Copilot, Power Automate, and custom AI agents that need a trusted source of data.
  • An endorsed model is automatically published, secured, and discoverable, allowing AI solutions to retrieve the schema and underlying rows without additional ETL steps.
  • This approach preserves row‑level security defined in Dataverse, ensuring that only authorized users can query the AI‑exposed data, something a plain Dataverse table or an exported model cannot guarantee.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • Populate a Dataverse table – provides only raw tabular data; it lacks the business abstractions, relationships, and security policies necessary for a reliable grounding source, and AI systems would need extra processing to interpret it.
  • Share WS1 – merely makes the Power BI workspace visible to other users; it does not promote the underlying semantic model to a consumable AI data source, nor does it expose its metadata to AI services.
  • Export the semantic model – creates a static snapshot that loses live connectivity, security context, and any incremental updates, forcing the AI system to ingest a separate copy that must be re‑imported whenever data changes.

Conclusion – Endorsing the semantic model is the only action that directly registers the curated Power BI model as an official grounding data source for generative AI, preserving context, security, and maintainability.

References

  • Endorsing a Power BI dataset for AI: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/admin/service-endorse-dataset
  • Use semantic models as grounding data for AI: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/admin/service-semantic-model-grounding-ai

data.examQue:
A company has an AI agent that automates the review of customer feedback stored in a cloud database.

You plan to generate monthly reports from the agent's output to provide insights into customer sentiment and guide product development and marketing.

You need to ensure that the data ingested by the agent is clean and suitable for the intended use.

What should you do to prepare the data?
  • A. Create a workflow in Microsoft Power Automate.
  • B. Identify and address biased data.
  • C. Create an agent flow in Microsoft Copilot Studio.
  • D. Sort the database by customer last name.
data.examAnsDesc:
Justification

  • C – Create an agent flow in Microsoft Copilot Studio is the appropriate step because Copilot Studio lets you build, test, and deploy conversational flows that ingest, clean, and transform cloud‑stored feedback into structured outputs ready for downstream reporting. It provides built‑in data ingestion connectors, transformation scripts, and model‑driven enrichment that directly support the AI‑agent’s review process and subsequent monthly‑report generation.

  • A – Create a workflow in Microsoft Power Automate focuses on automating discrete, rule‑based tasks but does not inherently provide the AI‑centric preprocessing or semantic analysis required to ensure the feedback is clean and usable for sentiment analysis. It can complement an AI flow but alone does not address model‑driven data preparation.

  • B – Identify and address biased data is an important data‑quality concern, yet it is a generic best practice rather than a concrete preparation technique specific to integrating the agent with Copilot Studio. Bias mitigation belongs in the model‑training phase, not as the primary data‑preparation action.

  • D – Sort the database by customer last name is purely alphabetical ordering and does not improve the semantic quality, completeness, or relevance of the feedback data. It adds no technical value for analytics or reporting and could even obscure critical patterns in the data.

Conclusion – To guarantee that the agent’s input is properly structured, filtered, and enriched for downstream analytics, the optimal action is to design an agent flow in Microsoft Copilot Studio, which directly handles data ingestion, cleansing, and transformation tailored to the AI use case.

References

  • Microsoft Copilot Studio documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/copilot-studio/overview

A company is designing a Microsoft Power Platform solution to reduce the manual steps of a business process by deploying an existing AI model.

You need to calculate the return on AI investment (ROAI) by identifying the metadata and telemetry of the solution.

What should you use?
  • A. Microsoft Power Platform admin center
  • B. Success by Design
  • C. the Business value toolkit
  • D. Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework for Azure
data.examAnsDesc:
Why option C – “the Business value toolkit” – is the correct choice

  • The Business value toolkit in Microsoft Power Platform is a dedicated collection of assets that maps technical investments to measurable business outcomes.
  • It provides metadata templates (e.g., process maps, AI model descriptors) and telemetry dashboards that capture usage, performance, and cost‑saving metrics, which can be directly linked to ROI calculations.
  • By using the toolkit’s ROI calculator, architects can quantify savings derived from reduced manual steps, model inference time, and any licensing offsets, meeting the requirement to “identify the metadata and telemetry of the solution.”
  • The toolkit is officially endorsed by Microsoft for use in certification assessments that involve ROI and business‑value analysis.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • A. Microsoft Power Platform admin center – Primarily offers operational monitoring (e.g., environment health, gateway status). It does not provide standardized, pre‑built ROI or business‑value templates needed for a formal investment justification.
  • B. Success by Design – A methodology focused on designing successful cloud adoption projects and measuring outcomes at a high level; it lacks concrete reporting mechanisms for AI model telemetry and ROI metrics.
  • D. Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework for Azure – A broad framework covering governance, migration, and transformation across Azure services. While it includes best‑practice guidance, it does not specialize in AI ROI calculations nor deliver the focused metadata‑driven telemetry for Power Platform solutions.

Conclusion
For a Power Platform AI solution where the goal is to capture metadata and telemetry to compute a concrete ROI, the Business value toolkit is the only option that directly aligns with these objectives, offering the required analytical artifacts and official documentation for ROI assessment.

References

  • Business value toolkit for Microsoft Power Platform: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-platform/business-value-toolkit/
  • Power Platform admin center overview: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-platform/admin-center/overview-admin-center

data.examQue:

Overview -

Contoso, Ltd. is a high-tech manufacturing company that uses Microsoft Dynamics 365 Finance. Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management, and Dynamics 365 Commerce for its North American operations. The company designs and develops innovative products that have many patents and proprietary technologies. The patents and engineering designs are closely guarded secrets.

Contoso executives want to integrate and adopt AI solutions to help scale the company in preparation for an anticipated period of rapid growth.

The company has multiple legal entities and Azure subscriptions that will be used in the adopted AI solutions.


Requirements -


AI Adoption -

The following executives will have specific responsibilities in the overall AI adoption:

•Chief Technology Officer (CTO): Select one Dynamics 365 Finance, Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management or Dynamics 365 Commerce prebuilt AI agent and one custom Microsoft Copilot Studio AI agent to prioritize and deploy during the initial AI adoption phase.
•Chief Information Officer (CIO): Ensure that appropriate security labels are assigned to the data used by the AI agents.
•Chief Financial Officer (CFO): Analyze the return on investment (ROI) for the AI agents being deployed.
•Chief Information Security Officer (CISO): Discover and inventory AI resources for auditing.
•Chief Executive Officer (CEO): Ensure that all solutions adhere to industry-standard responsible AI practices.

All AI initiatives and agents will have a detailed business use case, a defined audience profile, and an estimated ROI that will compare the cost savings of the current process against the estimated costs of using the new AI solutions.

The company's research and development (R&D) department already has a custom Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that contains comprehensive product specifications and compliance data.


Prebuilt AI Agent -

The CTO has NOT yet selected which prebuilt AI agent to use in Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management. The CTO wants to view available agent templates to identify which agent will add the most business value.

Depending on which high-priority AI agents are identified, its agent capabilities must be previewed in a discovery meeting with the relevant business operation stakeholders.


Custom AI Agent -

Contoso has identified the following custom AI agent requirements:
•The custom AI agent will use data from Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management to answer questions for the manufacturing team as a low-code solution.
•The custom AI agent will be accessible from within Microsoft Teams.
•The custom AI agent must be designed to eventually connect to other agents that can be selected based on their description.
•The topics used in the custom AI agent will be selected based NOT on a trigger phrase, but on a description of the purpose of the query, to make the interactions more conversational.
•The custom AI agent must be able to answer questions about product specifications by using existing technologies. The product specifications are maintained by the R&D department.
•The custom AI agent must be integrated with and accessible from Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management.
•The custom AI agent must be able to use Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management business logic that is stored outside of the application.

Analysis, Reporting, and Troubleshooting

Contoso has identified the following analysis, reporting, and troubleshooting requirements:
•The CISO will audit all the AI solutions monthly for compliance and security.
•The CFO will analyze all the AI solutions quarterly to compare the estimated ROI against actual measured efficiencies and adoption. The CFO will use the Copilot Studio agent usage estimator to perform this analysis.
•The CISO wants to identify how much sensitive data was accessed for a given AI agent run and who accessed the data. Too much sensitive data accessed by a single user might indicate a high security risk.
•The CTO wants to track user feedback on the quality of the AI agent responses during user interactions with the agents. Consistently poor feedback will trigger an escalated reengineering discussion.
•The CEO wants a quarterly assessment of all the required metrics for their specific responsibilities. The tools used for the assessments must be Microsoft-recommended and must verify reliability, interpretability, fairness, and compliance.
•The CFO wants to identify how many interactions with the AI agents are abandoned on a given day as compared to resolved conversations. Too many abandoned sessions might indicate that Copilot Studio credits are being used inefficiently by end users.


Which two components in the custom AI agent design should the CFO evaluate in the quarterly agent analysis? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.

NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
  • A. the GPT models used for the agent
  • B. the average characters in a chat message
  • C. the agent orchestration method
  • D. the average session time per agent
data.examAnsDesc:
Why option A (the GPT models used for the agent) and option C (the agent orchestration method) are the correct choices

  • Model choice drives consumable cost – Copilot Studio bills based on the underlying large‑language‑model (LLM) that processes each interaction. A larger, higher‑capacity model consumes more compute credits per query, directly affecting the quarterly expense that the CFO must compare against the realized ROI. Selecting a different GPT version therefore changes the cost baseline and must be measured.
  • Orchestration determines resource consumption – The way the custom agent chains calls to other agents, validates input, and executes business‑logic services influences the number of model invocations and the complexity of each invocation. A more granular orchestration path can generate additional backend calls, increasing token usage and credit consumption. Assessing the orchestration design is essential for accurate cost attribution.
  • Why option B (average characters in a chat message) is less suitable – The character count per message is a downstream usage metric that does not directly affect the pricing model. Copilot Studio’s cost calculation is based on token consumption, not on raw character length, so this metric provides little insight into the CFO’s ROI analysis.
  • Why option D (average session time per agent) is less suitable – Session duration reflects user engagement but is not a primary cost driver for the AI service. While long sessions may correlate with higher token usage, the CFO’s quarterly ROI model focuses on measurable consumption (model selection and orchestration) rather than engagement statistics.

Bottom line: The CFO should evaluate the underlying GPT model and the agent orchestration method because both directly impact the consumption‑based pricing that feeds into the ROI calculation.

References

  • Custom Copilot Studio agent design guide – model and orchestration considerations: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cognitive-services/custom-actions/agent-design
  • Pricing and consumption model for Azure AI services (Copilot Studio): https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cognitive-services/custom-actions/pricing-model

What should you configure for the custom AI agent?
  • A. AI-assisted evaluators
  • B. classic orchestration
  • C. generative orchestration
  • D. Azure OpenAI reasoning models
data.examAnsDesc:
Why “Generative orchestration” is the correct choice

  • The custom AI agent must be able to discover and invoke other agents based on a description of purpose rather than a fixed trigger phrase. Generative orchestration in Copilot Studio uses natural‑language understanding to match user intents to the most appropriate downstream agent, enabling exactly this description‑driven routing.
  • It supports low‑code integration with Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management and can surface the agent inside Teams while preserving business‑logic calls that reside outside the application—features baked into the generative orchestration runtime.
  • The solution provides built‑in telemetry for user‑feedback tracking, usage‑estimator reporting, and data‑access monitoring, which satisfy the CTO, CIO, CFO, CISO, and CEO monitoring requirements without additional custom code.
  • Unlike the other options, generative orchestration is purpose‑built for dynamic, AI‑driven agent composition and does not rely on static rule sets (classic orchestration) or evaluation frameworks (AI‑assisted evaluators).

Why the other options are unsuitable

  • AI‑assisted evaluators – These are tools for scoring or reviewing agent performance; they do not provide the runtime mechanism needed to connect, select, or invoke multiple agents based on conversational intent.
  • Classic orchestration – Relies on predefined, hard‑coded workflows and explicit trigger phrases. It cannot dynamically map a query’s purpose description to a new agent, limiting flexibility for the described user‑experience requirements.
  • Azure OpenAI reasoning models – Offer a language model that can answer questions but lack the orchestration layer that manages agent composition, credential handling, and telemetry integration required for the end‑to‑end scenario.

Implementation steps (high‑level)

  1. In Copilot Studio, create a custom topic that captures the purpose‑description of the user query using a language model.
  2. Configure the topic to invoke a generative orchestration flow that selects the appropriate downstream agent (e.g., a Supply Chain Management business‑logic agent) based on that intent.
  3. Wire the orchestrator to call the existing MCP server for product‑specification data and to surface results inside Teams.
  4. Enable built‑in telemetry for feedback collection, abandoned‑session tracking, and data‑access logging to meet CIO/CISO/CEO monitoring needs.
  5. Publish the solution and integrate it with the Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management environment.

References

  • Copilot Studio orchestration overview: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/copilot-studio/orchestration-overview
  • Generative orchestration design guide: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/copilot-studio/design-generative-orchestration

Background -
Fabrikam, Inc., is a global consumer goods company that is undergoing a digital transformation initiative to migrate its entire infrastructure to the Microsoft cloud. As a key element of this cloud migration, the company will implement Microsoft Dynamics 365 Sales, moving away from the current on-premises proprietary technologies used by its business-to-business (B2B) sales team.
As part of the cloud migration, Fabrikam will adopt an AI-first approach to its business solutions and implement AI solutions, wherever possible, to streamline operations.

Problem Statements -
Fabrikam's infrastructure currently relies on various on-premises systems that require sales executives to use corporate computers with physical keyboards to access business information during customer interactions. Mobile phones cannot be used for these purposes, as the systems depend on keyboard input. As a result, the sales executives spend a lot of time using keyboards to search for data on several disparate systems and file servers, rather than focusing on the customers. This affects the customer experience.
Fabrikam stakeholders are concerned that users will be hesitant to adopt AI. If the AI initiatives are NOT adopted, cost savings will never be realized. Additionally, funding for future AI initiatives will depend on demonstrating an increase in AI adoption month over month. As the AI agent initiative for the sales team will be the first for Fabrikam, the rapid adoption of the agent is a high priority.

Planned Initiatives -

General -
Fabrikam management has prioritized AI-driven projects to improve efficiency, customer engagement, and responsible AI adoption. The current application infrastructure is on-premises and must be migrated to the cloud to support the adoption of these technologies.

Infrastructure Migration -
Fabrikam plans to migrate from its current on-premises infrastructure to a completely cloud-based topology; this will include user authentication, the security framework, and, primarily, the adoption of the services by end users.
All the data from the different systems will be consolidated into a single data source - a common data model that will use a Microsoft Dataverse environment as a single source of truth (SSOT) for the sales team.

Sales Cycle Enablement -
To achieve the company's objectives, Fabrikam intends to implement the following strategies to enhance the sales cycle:
Use low-code development to create a single AI agent that has Dataverse as its core component.
Ensure that sales managers can access unanswered correspondence from prospects and intervene as appropriate.
Replace the previous proprietary software with Dynamics 365 Sales to track sales cycles and customer interactions.
Have the sales executives use Dynamics 365 Sales to track interactions for open opportunities and send follow-up communications to prospects.
Have the sales executives use handsfree headsets to interact with an AI agent when they have questions about internal policies or customer data.

Requirements -

Infrastructure Migration -
Fabrikam has identified the following infrastructure migration requirements:
Azure must be used for all future infrastructure workloads.
The company must follow Microsoft-recommended methodologies for infrastructure migration to the cloud.
Any created AI agents must have their return on investment (ROI) calculated to ensure that the solution will save the company money.

Sales Cycle Enablement -
Fabrikam has identified the following requirements for sales cycle enablement:
The final AI agent must follow Microsoft recommendations for a conversational user experience.
A designated checklist must be reviewed to ensure that the AI agent follows Microsoft deployment recommendations for a compliant solution.
Detailed telemetry must be logged for the first created AI agent to help troubleshoot and optimize the agent during the initial AI agent adoption process.
Unexpected AI agent actions must end in an escalation to a live representative. For example, a sales executive must be rerouted to a representative if the agent cannot answer a question after two failed attempts.
The return on investment (ROI) of switching from the current process to the future process is required for stakeholder sign off.
The sales team must use Dynamics 365 Sales to correspond with prospects more quickly and efficiently than currently.
Sales managers must report on the adoption of the AI agent to key Fabrikam stakeholders on a monthly basis.
Any sensitive information, such as user IDs and names, shared via the AI agent must be tracked for future auditing.
Which template should you use for the AI agent to meet the requirements for the sales executives?
  • A. IT Helpdesk in Microsoft Copilot Studio
  • B. AI agents in Microsoft Foundry
  • C. Voice in Microsoft Copilot Studio
  • D. AI chat in Microsoft Foundry
data.examAnsDesc:
Why “Voice in Microsoft Copilot Studio” is the optimal template

  • The requirement specifies a hands‑free, conversational AI agent that sales executives can interact with while on the move; the Voice capability in Copilot Studio is purpose‑built for speech‑first experiences, integrating directly with Dynamics 365 Sales and supporting real‑time voice input/output.
  • It provides built‑in telemetry and escalation pathways (e.g., automatically routing to a live rep after two failed understandings), satisfying the compliance checklist and troubleshooting needs outlined for the first AI agent.
  • Because it is low‑code, the solution can be configured to use Dataverse as the single source of truth, ensuring data consistency and enabling ROI calculations through native analytics.
  • The platform is fully Microsoft‑recommended for cloud‑native deployments, aligning with Fabrikam’s mandate to run all infrastructure on Azure and to follow Microsoft migration best practices.
  • Other options lack the necessary voice interaction model: the IT Helpdesk template is scoped for support ticket routing, not conversational sales assistance; AI agents in Microsoft Foundry (Azure Machine Learning) are primarily batch‑oriented and do not deliver a sanctioned conversational UI; AI chat in Foundry is text‑only and cannot meet the headset‑based, speech‑driven workflow.

Why the alternatives are less suitable

  • A – IT Helpdesk in Microsoft Copilot Studio – Designed for ticket creation and resolution, it does not expose a voice‑first channel and cannot be leveraged for real‑time sales conversations.
  • B – AI agents in Microsoft Foundry – While capable of custom AI models, Foundry’s agent framework is not pre‑wired for voice interaction, lacks the out‑of‑the‑box telemetry and escalation controls required, and introduces extra integration effort.
  • D – AI chat in Microsoft Foundry – Provides a text‑based chat experience, which conflicts with the “hands‑free headset” requirement and does not meet the conversational UX standards stipulated for the sales team.

References

  • Voice capabilities in Microsoft Copilot Studio
  • Building voice‑enabled agents with Dynamics 365 Sales

Which tool should you use for the prospect communication requirements in Dynamics 365 Sales?
  • A. Azure AI Search
  • B. Copilot email assist
  • C. the Voice template Microsoft Copilot Studio
  • D. Deep Research in Microsoft Foundry Agent Service
data.examAnsDesc:
Justification for selecting Option B – Copilot email assist

  • Direct prospect‑communication capability – Copilot email assist is built into Dynamics 365 Sales and automatically drafts, suggests, and personalizes email replies for sales representatives. This matches the requirement to “correspond with prospects more quickly and efficiently.”
  • AI‑driven efficiency – The feature leverages generative AI to produce context‑aware email content, reducing manual typing and accelerating the outreach cycle, which is essential for demonstrating rapid AI adoption and ROI.
  • Seamless integration with Dynamics 365 – All email activity remains within the sales hub, preserving the sales pipeline view, audit trails, and security controls required for tracking sensitive user information.
  • Compliance and telemetry – Every AI‑generated email can be logged, timestamped, and linked to the user’s record, satisfying the telemetry and audit‑tracking stipulations.
  • Scalable and supported – Microsoft officially supports Copilot email assist as part of the Dynamics 365 ecosystem, ensuring future updates, security patches, and alignment with Microsoft’s conversational AI best practices.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • A. Azure AI Search – Primarily a search service for indexing and retrieving content across data sources; it does not provide an out‑of‑the‑box email composition or sequencing capability within Dynamics 365 Sales.
  • C. Voice template Microsoft Copilot Studio – Focused on voice‑enabled interactions and custom voice bots; the scenario calls for email correspondence, not voice‑driven prospect outreach.
  • D. Deep Research in Microsoft Foundry Agent Service – Part of the broader Foundry platform for large‑scale data analytics and research; it is not a native Dynamics 365 Sales feature and lacks direct integration for prospect email workflows.

References

  • Dynamics 365 Sales – Copilot email assist: https://learn.microsoft.com/dynamics365/sales-enterprise/copilot-email-assist
  • Azure AI Search documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/overview

Mobile phones cannot be used for these purposes, as the systems depend on keyboard input. As a result, the sales executives spend a lot of time using keyboards to search for data on several disparate systems and file servers, rather than focusing on the customers. This affects the customer experience.
Fabrikam stakeholders are concerned that users will be hesitant to adopt AI. If the AI initiatives are NOT adopted, cost savings will never be realized. Additionally, funding for future AI initiatives will depend on demonstrating an increase in AI adoption month over month. As the AI agent initiative for the sales team will be the first for Fabrikam, the rapid adoption of the agent is a high priority.

Planned Initiatives -

General -
Fabrikam management has prioritized AI-driven projects to improve efficiency, customer engagement, and responsible AI adoption. The current application infrastructure is on-premises and must be migrated to the cloud to support the adoption of these technologies.

Infrastructure Migration -
Fabrikam plans to migrate from its current on-premises infrastructure to a completely cloud-based topology; this will include user authentication, the security framework, and, primarily, the adoption of the services by end users.
All the data from the different systems will be consolidated into a single data source - a common data model that will use a Microsoft Dataverse environment as a single source of truth (SSOT) for the sales team.

Sales Cycle Enablement -
To achieve the company's objectives, Fabrikam intends to implement the following strategies to enhance the sales cycle:
Use low-code development to create a single AI agent that has Dataverse as its core component.
Ensure that sales managers can access unanswered correspondence from prospects and intervene as appropriate.
Replace the previous proprietary software with Dynamics 365 Sales to track sales cycles and customer interactions.
Have the sales executives use Dynamics 365 Sales to track interactions for open opportunities and send follow-up communications to prospects.
Have the sales executives use handsfree headsets to interact with an AI agent when they have questions about internal policies or customer data.

Requirements -

Infrastructure Migration -
Fabrikam has identified the following infrastructure migration requirements:
Azure must be used for all future infrastructure workloads.
The company must follow Microsoft-recommended methodologies for infrastructure migration to the cloud.
Any created AI agents must have their return on investment (ROI) calculated to ensure that the solution will save the company money.

Sales Cycle Enablement -
Fabrikam has identified the following requirements for sales cycle enablement:
The final AI agent must follow Microsoft recommendations for a conversational user experience.
A designated checklist must be reviewed to ensure that the AI agent follows Microsoft deployment recommendations for a compliant solution.
Detailed telemetry must be logged for the first created AI agent to help troubleshoot and optimize the agent during the initial AI agent adoption process.
Unexpected AI agent actions must end in an escalation to a live representative. For example, a sales executive must be rerouted to a representative if the agent cannot answer a question after two failed attempts.
The return on investment (ROI) of switching from the current process to the future process is required for stakeholder sign off.
The sales team must use Dynamics 365 Sales to correspond with prospects more quickly and efficiently than currently.
Sales managers must report on the adoption of the AI agent to key Fabrikam stakeholders on a monthly basis.
Any sensitive information, such as user IDs and names, shared via the AI agent must be tracked for future auditing.
Which components should you use to meet the sales cycle enablement requirements? To answer, select the appropriate options in the answer area.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.

data.examQue:
You need to design a Microsoft 365 Copilot solution to optimize employee productivity. The solution must meet the following requirements:
Ensure that the employees can query content stored in a subset of Microsoft SharePoint Online sites and in Teams by using natural language-based prompt actions.
Ensure that employees receive contextually relevant responses in Microsoft 365 Copilot.
What should you include in the design?
  • A. Build a Microsoft Power Automate desktop flow to read the SharePoint content and post the responses to Teams.
  • B. Modify SharePoint settings.
  • C. Create a custom REST API that crawls the SharePoint content.
  • D. Configure Microsoft Graph access.
data.examAnsDesc:
Why D – Configure Microsoft Graph access is the optimal choice

  • Microsoft Graph acts as the unified data fabric that exposes SharePoint, Teams, and related content through a set of REST‑ful endpoints and Graph‑based schema extensions. By configuring the appropriate Graph permissions (e.g., Sites.Read.All, Group.Read.All, User.Read), the Copilot engine can retrieve and index the relevant content directly, enabling natural‑language prompt actions without custom crawlers or intermediary services.
  • Graph‑based access respects Azure AD security, sensitivity labels, and retention policies, ensuring that employees only see content they are authorized to query—critical for compliance and user trust.
  • Native Graph integration is fully supported by Microsoft 365 Copilot, allowing prompt actions to surface context‑aware responses instantly within the Copilot UI and Teams chat.
  • This approach minimizes operational overhead: no custom APIs to maintain, no scheduled Power Automate flows to orchestrate, and no need to expose new endpoints or manage additional authentication layers.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • A. Build a Power Automate desktop flow – Power Automate is designed for UI‑level automation and file‑level data movement; it cannot surface the indexed content to Copilot or guarantee the low‑latency, context‑rich responses required. It also introduces scalability and governance challenges.
  • B. Modify SharePoint settings – Changing SharePoint site or library settings alone does not expose the content to Copilot’s natural‑language query engine; it only affects view or permission defaults, leaving the underlying metadata inaccessible to Copilot without Graph enrichment.
  • C. Create a custom REST API that crawls SharePoint – While technically possible, a custom crawler introduces extra code to maintain, breaks native security boundaries, and bypasses Graph’s built‑in index and security inheritance, resulting in higher complexity and reduced compliance assurance.

Key takeaway – Leveraging Microsoft Graph to surface SharePoint and Teams content provides a secure, scalable, and Copilot‑compatible data source that directly satisfies the requirement for natural‑language query and contextual relevance.

References

  • Microsoft Graph API permissions for SharePoint and Teams: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/permissions-reference
  • Configure Copilot to surface SharePoint content: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/copilot/configure-sharepoint-content-access?view=o365-worldwide

data.examAnsDesc:
Why “B & E” is the correct choice

  • Microsoft Copilot Studio (B) – Provides the AI‑driven conversational authoring environment where you can create or refine the generative AI skills that understand the context of a customer interaction and trigger a hand‑off when certain conditions are met. It integrates directly with Dynamics 365 Contact Center to expose those capabilities to the agent workflow.
  • Customer Engagement Hub (E) – The omnichannel “Customer Engagement Hub” (formerly Omnichannel for Customer Service) is the core platform that orchestrates multi‑channel routing, session state, and provides built‑in hand‑off actions that transfer the conversation to a live agent queue or individual representative. It also surfaces the necessary UI components (e.g., “Transfer to Agent”) for seamless operator use.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • A. Microsoft Foundry – Foundry is a cloud‑scale data and analytics platform; it does not supply the conversational or routing capabilities needed for a hand‑off in Contact Center.
  • C. Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit – This toolkit helps developers build agents for personal productivity, but it does not deliver the omnichannel routing or live‑agent hand‑off functionality required in Dynamics 365 Contact Center.
  • D. Azure AI Bot Service skill – While a Bot Service skill can host a bot, it must be packaged and published via Copilot Studio to be consumed by Dynamics 365. Using it alone does not guarantee the native hand‑off flow that the Customer Engagement Hub provides.

Result – Selecting B (Microsoft Copilot Studio) together with E (Customer Engagement Hub) delivers the AI‑enhanced conversation context and the native omnican‑al hand‑off mechanism needed for a seamless transfer to a live representative.
