data.examQue:
A company has a Microsoft Copilot Studio agent that provides answers based on a knowledge base for customer support.
Users report that, occasionally, the agent provides inaccurate answers.
You need to use metrics from the Analytics tab in Copilot Studio to identify the cause of the inaccuracies.
Which two options should you use? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
  • A. survey results
  • B. session information and session outcomes
  • C. topic usage and topics with low resolution
  • D. engagement, resolution, and escalation rates
  • E. quality of generated answers
data.examAnsDesc:
Why options C and D are the correct choices

  • Topic usage and topics with low resolution (C) – In Copilot Studio’s Analytics tab, the Topic Usage metric shows how often a specific knowledge‑base topic is invoked, while Topics with low resolution highlights those that frequently fail to achieve a confident answer. Low‑resolution topics often map to the instances where the agent delivers inaccurate or ambiguous responses, making this a direct indicator of the root cause.
  • Session information and session outcomes (D) – Session‑level analytics provide detailed telemetry such as user‑question phrasing, confidence scores, escalation triggers, and whether the generated answer was accepted or corrected by the user. These metrics reveal patterns (e.g., repeated escalations or low confidence scores) that pinpoint the conditions under which inaccuracies occur.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • Survey results (A) – These reflect user satisfaction post‑interaction but do not surface the precise technical reasons (e.g., low‑confidence scores or unresolved topics) that cause inaccuracies during the conversation.
  • Engagement, resolution, and escalation rates (B) – Engagement and resolution metrics are useful for overall bot performance, yet they are not granular enough to isolate the accuracy problem; escalation rates merely signal that a human was needed, not why the answer was wrong.
  • (E) Quality of generated answers – While “quality” is the outcome we care about, Copilot Studio does not expose a standalone “quality” metric in the Analytics tab; quality must be inferred from the underlying session and topic metrics listed above. Hence it is not a selectable option for the analytics‑tab investigation.

References

  • Copilot Studio Analytics Overview
  • Using Topic Insights to Improve Accuracy

data.examQue:
A company uses a fine-tuned Microsoft Foundry model that requires frequent updates as new customer feedback becomes available.
You need to design an application lifecycle management (ALM) process that meets the following requirements:
Data changes must be tracked and versioned.
The model must be retrained consistently by using approved training data.
Which two actions should you include in the design? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
  • A. Associate the storage location to the fine-tuning job.
  • B. Create a content filter.
  • C. Store the training data in Azure Files.
  • D. Upload the training data to Microsoft Foundry data files
  • E. Store the training data in Azure Blob Storage that has version control enabled.
data.examAnsDesc:
• D. Upload the training data to Microsoft Foundry data files – Microsoft Foundry stores model‑training artifacts in dedicated data files that are version‑controlled, immutable, and linked directly to the fine‑tuning job. This ensures that each training iteration is recorded, reproducible, and can be reviewed or rolled back.
  • E. Store the training data in Azure Blob Storage that has version control enabled – Azure Blob Storage with versioning preserves every change to the data set (e.g., new feedback files) as immutable versions. When used as the source for Foundry data files, it provides traceable, auditable data provenance that satisfies “data changes must be tracked and versioned.”

Why the other options are unsuitable

  • A. Associate the storage location to the fine‑tuning job – Simply associating a location does not enforce versioning or guarantee that the data is stored in a managed, auditable repository; it lacks explicit version‑control semantics.
  • B. Create a content filter – A content filter controls what data is processed (e.g., profanity removal) but does not address versioning of training data or ensure consistent retraining against approved data.
  • C. Store the training data in Azure Files – Azure Files does not provide built‑in object‑level versioning for large data sets; it is better suited for traditional file shares, not for the immutable, version‑tracked data required by Foundry model pipelines.

References

  • Microsoft Foundry – Managing Training Data
  • https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-foundry-manage-data
  • Azure Blob Storage – Versioning
  • https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/storage-blob-versioning

data.examQue:
A company has an AI solution that uses Azure OpenAI models.
You need to recommend a governance solution that monitors and audits changes to model configurations and data usage. The solution must minimize administrative effort.
What should you include in the recommendation?
  • A. Azure Monitor
  • B. Azure Stream Analytics
  • C. Azure API Management
  • D. Azure Policy
  • E. Microsoft Purview
data.examAnsDesc:
• Azure Monitor
  • Provides built‑in telemetry collection, alerting, and dashboard capabilities for Azure resources, including Azure OpenAI Service.
  • Enables continuous monitoring of model configuration changes (e.g., deployment revisions, endpoint updates) and usage patterns (token consumption, request latency).
  • Reduces administrative overhead by automatically aggregating logs from Azure Monitor diagnostics and supporting out‑of‑the‑box alerts and workbooks that can be reused across subscriptions.
  • Azure Stream Analytics
  • Designed for real‑time stream processing; it adds unnecessary complexity when the requirement is simple monitoring and auditing rather than complex event‑driven transformations.
  • Azure API Management
  • Focuses on API lifecycle management, throttling, and versioning, not on holistic governance of model configuration changes or data lineage across OpenAI deployments.
  • Azure Policy
  • Enforces compliance rules but does not provide continuous monitoring or auditing capabilities; it must be paired with other services to capture change history.
  • Microsoft Purview
  • Offers enterprise‑wide data governance and lineage, yet its integration with Azure OpenAI is indirect and typically requires custom connectors, increasing admin effort compared to Azure Monitor’s native monitoring features.

Because Azure Monitor directly delivers continuous, low‑maintenance telemetry and alerting for both model configuration drift and data usage, it best satisfies the requirement to monitor and audit changes while minimizing administrative overhead.

References

  • Azure Monitor documentation for Azure OpenAI: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-monitor/insights/azure-openai-monitor
  • Monitoring and troubleshooting Azure OpenAI Service: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/monitor-azure-openai-service

data.examQue:
You are evaluating a Microsoft Copilot Studio agent that supports Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Service representatives.
You need to recommend a testing solution that meets the following requirements:
Evaluates agent effectiveness during active sessions
Validates whether the agent delivers accurate and helpful responses
Provides measurable, actionable insights for continuous improvement
What should you recommend?
  • A. Track resolution, deflection, and accuracy by using dashboards and use scripts to ensure consistent responses.
  • B. Perform load testing to validate how the agent scales under a high chat volume.
  • C. Review historical tickets to find agents that have the shortest resolution times.
  • D. Measure uptime and page load times.
data.examAnsDesc:
Why option A is the correct choice

  • Active‑session evaluation – Copilot Studio provides real‑time telemetry dashboards that capture resolution rate, deflection rate, and response accuracy while the conversation is occurring.
  • Accuracy & helpfulness validation – Scripted checkpoints can be embedded to compare the agent’s output against predefined knowledge‑base answers, flagging deviations instantly.
  • Measurable, actionable insights – The captured metrics are quantifiable (e.g., % of sessions resolved without human hand‑off, average deflection score) and can be fed back into model‑tuning loops for continuous improvement.
  • Alignment with Microsoft best‑practice testing – Microsoft’s guidance for Copilot Studio emphasizes continuous monitoring through dashboards and scripted validation to ensure the agent behaves as intended during live interactions.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • Option B (load testing) – Focuses on scalability under high volume but does not assess response quality or accuracy during a session.
  • Option C (historical ticket shortest resolution times) – Relies on past incident data and does not evaluate the current agent’s performance in live conversations.
  • Option D (uptime and page load times) – Measures infrastructure performance only; it provides no insight into the semantics or helpfulness of the agent’s replies.

Hence, option A best satisfies the requirement to evaluate effectiveness during active sessions, validate accuracy/helpfulness, and deliver actionable analytics.

References

  • Copilot Studio – Monitoring & analytics: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/copilot-studio/monitoring-analytics
  • Dynamics 365 Customer Service – Agent performance dashboards: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dynamics365/customer-service/analytics/performance-dashboards

data.examQue:
A company uses multiple Microsoft Copilot Studio agents across different channels.
You need to recommend a monitoring solution that provides comprehensive telemetry data and performance insights for the agents.
What should you include in the recommendation?
  • A. Application Insights
  • B. Azure Advisor
  • C. Azure DevOps
  • D. Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Voice
data.examAnsDesc:
Answer: A – Application Insights

Why Application Insights is the optimal choice

  • Built‑in telemetry for Copilot Studio agents – Application Insights automatically captures request, dependency, and custom trace telemetry for Azure‑hosted bot services and AI agents, enabling end‑to‑end performance monitoring and latency analysis.
  • Real‑time dashboards & alerts – It provides built‑in metrics (e.g., request rate, failure rates, response times) and configurable alerts that can be linked to agent health checks, supporting proactive incident response.
  • Deep analytics & AI‑driven insights – Integrated with Azure Monitor workbooks and AI capabilities (anomaly detection, usage patterns), it offers the comprehensive insight required for capacity planning and optimization of multiple agents across channels.
  • Native integration with Azure resources – Since Copilot Studio agents run on Azure Bot Service and AI resources, Application Insights is the default monitoring service for those workloads, ensuring seamless data collection without additional configuration.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • B. Azure Advisor – Provides prescriptive recommendations for cost, security, and performance, but it does not collect or visualize telemetry data from running agents; it is advisory rather than monitoring.
  • C. Azure DevOps – Primarily supports source control, CI/CD pipelines, and work‑item tracking; while it can host test results, it lacks dedicated telemetry‑focused monitoring for AI agents and does not provide the granular performance metrics needed for production supervision.
  • D. Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Voice – A survey and feedback collection tool for gathering customer responses; it does not offer system‑level telemetry or performance monitoring capabilities for Copilot agents.

Conclusion – To obtain comprehensive telemetry, real‑time performance insights, and actionable alerts for multiple Copilot Studio agents, Application Insights is the only option that directly serves those monitoring requirements.

References

  • Application Insights for Azure Bot Service: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-monitor/app/azure-bot-service
  • Monitoring Azure AI resources with Application Insights: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/monitor/app/monitor-azure-resources

data.examQue:
A company has an AI solution built by using Microsoft Copilot Studio and Power Platform. The solution is used by the company's sales, marketing, and customer service teams.
You are performing a return on AI investment (ROAI) analysis to evaluate the impact of the solution.
You need to identify which measurable business drivers to include in the analysis.
Which two business drivers should you identify? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
  • A. the reduced average case resolution time
  • B. market capitalization
  • C. economic market predictability
  • D. increased employee productivity
  • E. brand awareness
data.examAnsDesc:
Why the correct choices (A & D) are the most appropriate business drivers

  • Reduced average case resolution time (A) – This metric is directly tied to the AI‑enabled automation that Copilot Studio and Power Platform deliver to customer‑service workflows. Faster case closure translates into lower handling costs, higher customer‑satisfaction scores, and measurable time‑saved per incident, all of which can be quantified in monetary terms for an ROI model.

  • Increased employee productivity (D) – AI‑driven assistants in sales, marketing, and service automate routine tasks (e.g., lead scoring, content generation, ticket triage). Productivity gains are expressed as additional deals closed, campaigns launched, or tickets resolved per employee hour, providing a clear quantitative contribution to the investment’s financial return.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • Market capitalization (B) – This is a macro‑level market metric that reflects overall company valuation and is influenced by many factors beyond the AI solution. It is not a controllable or attributable business driver for a specific AI implementation.

  • Economic market predictability (C) – While market conditions affect revenue, they are external and largely out of the control of the AI project team. ROI analyses focus on drivers that the organization can influence through its technology investments.

  • Brand awareness (E) – Brand perception can be impacted by AI‑generated content, but measuring its direct financial impact is indirect and often subjective. ROI calculations require clear, quantifiable cost‑savings or revenue uplift, which brand awareness alone does not provide.

Key takeaway for the exam – When evaluating ROAI, select business drivers that are directly measurable, attributable to the AI solution, and convertible into monetary value (e.g., time saved, productivity gains). Drivers that are external, macro‑economic, or only loosely related to operational outcomes are not appropriate for a focused ROI analysis.

References

  • Microsoft Learn: Measuring AI ROI with Power Platform and Copilot Studio – https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-platform/ai/measure-roi
  • Copilot Studio documentation: Business outcomes and ROI – https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/copilot-studio/plan/business-outcomes

data.examQue:
A company has a Microsoft Foundry agent that summarizes customer feedback and recommends products to customers. The agent references data from multiple knowledge sources.

Users report that the agent response time is slow.

Telemetry data shows that the agent frequently reaches its token usage limit.

You need to recommend a solution to reduce token usage without degrading the quality of the generated responses.

What should you recommend?
  • A. Chunk documents during indexing.
  • B. Reduce the number of knowledge sources used by the agent.
  • C. Reconfigure the prompts to limit the amount of retrieved content from the knowledge sources.
  • D. Lower the maximum token usage limit for the responses.
data.examAnsDesc:
Correct answer: C

Explanation:

  • Option A (chunk documents during indexing) may improve retrieval relevance but does not directly limit the amount of content fed to the model at inference time, so it does not address the token‑limit problem.
  • Option B (reduce the number of knowledge sources) could shrink the retrieval set but risks losing valuable context that improves answer quality, potentially degrading usefulness.
  • Option C (reconfigure prompts to limit retrieved content) directly controls the size of the context window by restricting how many tokens are included from the knowledge sources, thereby reducing token consumption while preserving response quality through selective retrieval.
  • Option D (lower the maximum token usage limit) merely caps output length without improving the input efficiency; it may cut off useful content and does not affect token usage of the retrieved material.

References:
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/search/ How to set up retrieval with RAG in Azure AI Search
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/ How to tune prompts and control token usage in Azure OpenAI completions

A company has Microsoft Power Platform development, staging, and production environments. Each environment has its own Microsoft Dataverse tables and Azure AI Search index.

You are designing an application lifecycle management (AIM) process to deploy a Microsoft Copilot Studio agent between the environments.

The company has a Copilot Studio agent named Agent1 in development. Agent1 uses the following grounding data sources:

•A Dataverse table named CustomerOrders
•An Azure AI Search index named customer-knowledge

You need to deploy Agent1 to production. The solution must ensure that the agent uses the production grounding data sources, minimizes downtime, and handles credentials and endpoints securely.

What should you include in the deployment package solution, and what should you reconfigure after the deployment? To answer, select the appropriate options in the answer area.

NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
data.examAnsDesc:
Agent1 and references to the data sources

Logic: When moving a Copilot agent between environments (e.g., from Development to Production), the solution package must contain the agent itself and the Connection References it relies on.

Connection References vs. Connections: You do not include the actual "connections" (which contain specific user credentials or secrets) in the deployment package. Instead, you include a "reference" or placeholder that points to the type of data source needed.

Exclusion of Data Sources: The actual underlying data sources (like a SQL database or a specific SharePoint site) are external to the solution and are not "packaged" inside it.

Reconfigure after the deployment

The environment variables

Logic: Environment variables are used to store parameter keys and values that are unique to a specific environment (such as a URL for a development site vs. a production site).

Post-Deployment Step: After importing a solution into a new environment, you must manually provide or update the values for these environment variables to ensure the agent interacts with the correct local resources.

Security: This practice ensures that sensitive configuration details or environment-specific endpoints are not hard-coded into the agent, allowing for a "build once, deploy many" architecture.

data.examQue:
A company has a Microsoft Copilot Studio agent that uses generative AI to assist Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Service representatives.

The agent currently exhibits a low resolution rate and a high escalation rate.

You need to identify the issue.

What should you use?
  • A. the Agent dashboard of Dynamics 365 Customer Service historical analytics
  • B. the Insights tab from the Search & intelligence settings of the Microsoft 365 admin center
  • C. the Copilot hub in the Power Platform admin center
  • D. the Analytics tab in Copilot Studio
data.examAnsDesc:
Why Option D is the correct choice

  • The Analytics tab in Copilot Studio provides real‑time, role‑specific metrics for the Copilot agent, including resolution rate, intent accuracy, and escalation trends. These insights directly surface the gaps that cause the observed low resolution and high escalation rates.
  • By reviewing the interaction breakdown (session success, confidence scores, fallback triggers) you can pinpoint the exact dialog paths or knowledge gaps that lead to premature escalations.
  • Adjusting the agent’s knowledge base, prompt flows, or clarification thresholds based on this analytics view allows you to increase successful autonomous resolutions and reduce the need for human hand‑off.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • Option A – Agent dashboard of Dynamics 365 Customer Service historical analytics
    This dashboard focuses on end‑user service performance (case volume, handle time, SLA) rather than the underlying AI model behavior of the Copilot agent. It does not expose the granular confidence or intent‑recognition metrics needed to diagnose why the bot fails to resolve queries.

  • Option B – Insights tab from the Search & intelligence settings of the Microsoft 365 admin center
    This area monitors search usage and AI‑enhanced content discovery across the tenant but lacks the specific Copilot‑agent telemetry (e.g., turn‑by‑turn response quality, escalation triggers) required for targeted remediation of the Customer Service bot.

  • Option C – Copilot hub in the Power Platform admin center
    While the Power Platform hub offers a high‑level view of all Copilot deployments, it aggregates usage across all agents and does not drill into the detailed analytical metrics available in the Copilot Studio Analytics view for a particular agent instance.

Conclusion
Only the Analytics tab in Copilot Studio equips you with the precise, agent‑specific performance data needed to diagnose and remediate the low resolution and high escalation issues in the Dynamics 365 Customer Service Copilot.

References

  • Copilot Studio analytics overview – https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-platform/copilot-studio/analytics-overview
  • Dynamics 365 Customer Service analytics – https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dynamics365/customer-service/analytics/analytics-overview

data.examQue:
A company has a Microsoft Foundry generative AI model.

You need to evaluate the model's output to measure the overall quality and coherence of generated responses. The evaluation must use GPT-4o as a judge and return a numeric score for each output.

Which type of metric should you use?
  • A. AI quality (NLP)
  • B. risk and safety
  • C. Groundedness
  • D. AI quality (AI assisted)
data.examAnsDesc:
Technical Justification

  • Metric Type: D – AI quality (AI assisted) – This category is expressly designed for evaluating the overall quality and coherence of generative‑AI outputs by using an external large‑language model (e.g., GPT‑4o) as a judge that produces a numeric rating. It captures fluency, logical flow, contextual relevance, and overall user‑perceived quality, which are precisely the dimensions the question asks to measure.

  • Why the other options are less appropriate

    • A. AI quality (NLP) – Focuses primarily on intrinsic NLP metrics such as perplexity, grammar, or lexical diversity. It does not involve an autonomous external judge returning a score.
    • B. Risk and safety – Concerns detecting harmful, biased, or unsafe content. While important, it does not assess coherence or overall quality of the response.
    • C. Groundedness – Measures whether the model’s output is supported by evidence from a knowledge base or retrieval corpus. It evaluates factual alignment, not holistic response quality or the ability of GPT‑4o to rank/score responses.

Conclusion – To obtain a numeric quality score from an AI judge (GPT‑4o) that reflects the model’s overall output quality, the AI quality (AI assisted) metric is the only option that aligns with the described requirements.

References

  1. Microsoft Foundry – Evaluating Generative AI Outputs Using AI‑Assisted Quality Metrics
    https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/evaluation/ai-assisted-quality

  2. GPT‑4o as a Judging Model for Synthetic Response Scoring
    https://openai.com/research/gpt-4o-judging-guide

data.examQue:
A company plans to deploy a Microsoft Foundry agent.

You need to recommend an application lifecycle management (ALM) process to ensure that the agent evaluates against baseline accuracy metrics before being deployed.

What should you recommend?
  • A. Configure GitHub Actions for new agent versions.
  • B. Deploy each new agent version directly to production.
  • C. Use Observability in Foundry Control Plane with evaluation and drift monitoring.
  • D. Enable Application Insights and use Azure Monitor.
data.examAnsDesc:
Why option C is the optimal choice

  • Integrated evaluation & drift monitoring: Foundry’s Control Plane includes built‑in observability that continuously measures model accuracy against a predefined baseline and detects performance drift in real‑time. This ensures that any newly trained agent is validated before it reaches production.
  • Automated gate‑keeping: The observability layer can be wired to an ALM gate that blocks promotion to production until the accuracy threshold (e.g., ≥ 95 % of baseline) is met and no significant drift is detected. This creates a data‑driven, repeatable release gate without manual inspection.
  • End‑to‑end lifecycle support: It aligns with the full ALM pipeline—continuous integration, testing, staging, and deployment—by leveraging native Foundry tooling rather than relying on generic CI/CD or monitoring services.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • A. Configure GitHub Actions for new agent versions – GitHub Actions can automate provisioning, but it does not provide built‑in accuracy evaluation or drift detection; additional custom logic would be required to enforce the baseline check.
  • B. Deploy each new agent version directly to production – Direct deployment bypasses any validation step, exposing the production environment to unverified performance and making rollback or monitoring reactive rather than preventive.
  • D. Enable Application Insights and use Azure Monitor – While Azure Monitor offers robust telemetry, it is external to Foundry and does not natively expose model‑specific accuracy metrics or drift alerts within the Foundry ecosystem, requiring extra integration effort.

Thus, leveraging Foundry’s native Observability in Control Plane with evaluation and drift monitoring delivers a purpose‑built, automated verification gate that fits cleanly into an ALM workflow and satisfies the requirement to assess accuracy before deployment.

References

  • Foundry Observability Documentation – https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/foundry/observability
  • Control Plane Overview & Monitoring Features – https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/foundry/control-plane

data.examQue:
You need to recommend a security solution for agents in a Microsoft Power Platform environment.

The agents must use only approved connectors and services. The solution must prevent the agents from accessing sensitive data.

What should you recommend?
  • A. Configure Azure Monitor to capture connector activity logs.
  • B. Enable a Microsoft Dataverse audit.
  • C. Deploy data loss prevention (DLP) policies in Power Platform.
  • D. Enable customer-managed keys in Microsoft Dataverse.
data.examAnsDesc:
Justification

  • Correct choice – C. Deploy data loss prevention (DLP) policies in Power Platform

    • DLP policies enforce that only approved connectors and services may be used and can block actions that attempt to read, write, or export sensitive data from Dataverse or other sources.
    • They operate at the platform level, automatically inspecting data classifications and blocking unauthorized transfers, which directly satisfies the requirement to “prevent the agents from accessing sensitive data.”
    • Configuring DLP is the standard, supported method recommended by Microsoft to regulate data access for Power Platform agents while adhering to approved connector usage.

  • Why the other options are less suitable

    • A. Configure Azure Monitor to capture connector activity logs – Azure Monitor provides visibility and alerting but does not prevent access; it only records events after they occur, leaving sensitive data exposed during the activity.
    • B. Enable a Microsoft Dataverse audit – Auditing logs help trace who accessed data, but they do not restrict or block unauthorized access, so they cannot enforce the “only approved connectors” rule.
    • D. Enable customer‑managed keys in Microsoft Dataverse – Customer‑managed keys control encryption at rest, but they do not limit which connectors or services can invoke the data; they only protect the data encrypted on disk.

Conclusion
Deploying DLP policies is the only option that directly enforces approved connector usage and actively blocks attempts to access or exfiltrate sensitive data by Power Platform agents, making it the optimal recommendation.

References

  • Microsoft Learn – Data loss prevention policies in Power Platform
  • Microsoft Docs – Secure your Power Platform environment with DLP policies

data.examQue:
A company has an AI business solution that uses Microsoft Copilot Studio agents.

You need to recommend prompt best practices to improve the effectiveness of agent interactions.

Which two actions should you include in the recommendation? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.

NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
  • A. Track the duration of the average user session.
  • B. Analyze the prompt length distribution.
  • C. Regularly test and refine the prompts based on user input.
  • D. Use clear and specific instructions in the prompts.
  • E. Measure system resource usage during prompt processing.
data.examAnsDesc:
Justification

  • C – Regularly test and refine the prompts based on user input
    Continuous evaluation of prompt performance is a core best‑practice in Copilot Studio. By capturing user feedback, error rates, and satisfaction metrics, developers can iteratively adjust wording, tone, and logic to eliminate ambiguities and boost task success rates. This systematic refinement directly aligns with the principle of prompt optimization through empirical validation.

  • D – Use clear and specific instructions in the prompts
    Well‑crafted prompts that state the desired role, context, steps, and output format reduce model confusion and hallucination. Clear, concise instructions enable the underlying language model to generate accurate, relevant responses and improve user trust. This is the foundational guidance for effective prompt design in Microsoft’s documentation.

Why the other options are less suitable

  • A – Track the duration of the average user session – Session length is a usability metric but does not directly reflect prompt quality; it can be influenced by many external factors unrelated to prompt design.
  • B – Analyze the prompt length distribution – While longer prompts may affect token consumption, length alone is not a reliable indicator of effectiveness; content relevance and clarity are more important.
  • E – Measure system resource usage during prompt processing – Resource utilization is an infrastructure concern; it informs scaling and cost, not the conversational efficacy of the prompts.

References

  • Prompt engineering guidance for Copilot Studio: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/copilot-studios/prompt-engineering
  • Best practices for designing effective prompts in Microsoft AI: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/cognitive-services/prompt-best-practices

Conclusion: To enhance agent interaction effectiveness, the solution must emphasize iterative testing (C) and clear, specific prompt wording (D). These actions directly target prompt quality, whereas the other options address peripheral or unrelated performance aspects.

data.examQue:
A company has Microsoft Copilot Studio agents.

The company plans to deploy custom connectors across development, test and production environments.

You need to design an application lifecycle management (ALM) process to ensure consistency and prevent direct editing in production.

Which two actions should you include in the design? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.

NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
  • A. Deploy managed solutions to production.
  • B. Deploy unmanaged solutions to production.
  • C. Manually rebuild the agents in each environment.
  • D. Move the agents between the environments by using data export and import.
  • E. Include agents and connectors in a solution.
data.examAnsDesc:
Justification

  • Include agents and connectors in a solution (A) – Packaging the Copilot Studio agents and their custom connectors within an unmanaged or managed solution makes the entire automation artifact a first‑class, version‑controlled entity. This ensures that any change to the agent is captured as part of a solution import, prevents ad‑hoc edits in production, and aligns with ALM best practices for reproducibility and auditability.

  • Move the agents between the environments by using data export and import (D) – Exporting the solution from the development or test environment and importing it into production propagates the same artifact across the lifecycle without recreating configuration manually. Export‑import respects environment‑specific parameters (e.g., connection strings, environment variables) and provides a controlled, auditable migration path, eliminating the risk of divergent customizations.

Why the other options are unsuitable

  • Deploy unmanaged solutions to production (B) – Unmanaged solutions allow uncontrolled modifications and can introduce breaking changes in production, violating the goal of a governed ALM process. Managed solutions are preferred for production because they enforce versioning and prevent accidental overwrites.

  • Manually rebuild the agents in each environment (C) – Manual rebuilds are error‑prone, time‑consuming, and do not capture the full solution context (metadata, dependencies, version history). They also bypass the solution‑based deployment pipeline, leading to inconsistencies and making rollback or audit trails impossible.

  • Move the agents between the environments by using data export and import (E) is not required – While exporting/importing is part of the process, it must be done through a solution artifact (option D). Simply moving raw agent files bypasses solution lifecycle tools and undermines governance.

Conclusion – The design should (A) embed agents and connectors in a solution and (D) move them across environments via solution export‑import, ensuring consistency, version control, and safe production deployment.

References

  • Copilot Studio – Managing solutions: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/copilot-studio/about-solutions
  • Export and import solutions: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powerapps/maker/data-platform/export-import-solutions

It is the recommended monitoring service for Copilot Studio agents because it can ingest custom telemetry emitted by the agents and deliver real‑time performance insights, alerts, and root‑cause analysis across multiple channels.

  • Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Voice (B) – A survey/feedback collection tool; it captures user sentiment but does not expose the granular operational metrics (latency, error rates, token usage) required for monitoring agent performance.

  • Log Analytics (C) – A query engine for aggregating logs across Azure services, yet it relies on a pre‑populated data source. For Copilot Studio, the telemetry source is specifically Application Insights, and using Log Analytics alone does not give the out‑of‑the‑box agent‑specific insights and visualizations.

  • Microsoft Purview (D) – Focused on data governance, classification, and compliance; it does not provide performance monitoring or telemetry dashboards for conversational agents.

Conclusion: Application Insights is the only option that delivers comprehensive, real‑time telemetry and performance analytics directly supported by Microsoft Copilot Studio, ensuring reliable monitoring across all deployed agents.

data.examQue:
A company has an AI solution named Solution1 that is deployed to the production environment. Solution1 uses an Azure OpenAI model to generate marketing emails for existing customers.

During an internal review, you identify that Solution1 creates different emails depending on the customers’ traits.

You need to recommend a strategy to mitigate the bias. The strategy must adhere to Microsoft responsible AI principles.

What should you recommend?
  • A. Modify Solution1 to randomly generate emails for different traits.
  • B. Modify the system instructions of Solution1.
  • C. Retrain the model by using a larger dataset.
  • D. Modify the contents of the training dataset.
data.examAnsDesc:
Technical Justification

  • Option B – Modify the system instructions of Solution1 is the most appropriate corrective action.

    • By adjusting the system‑level prompts that guide the Azure OpenAI model, you directly influence how the model interprets and applies customer traits when generating emails. This aligns with Microsoft’s Responsible AI pillar of Fairness, allowing you to embed bias‑mitigation rules, fairness constraints, or explicit de‑biasing language into the system instructions without retraining the model.
    • It provides a controllable, low‑risk way to enforce that the output respects ethical and fairness policies (e.g., avoiding stereotypical language, ensuring equitable tone).
    • The change can be implemented and tested incrementally, making it easier to audit and validate that the desired bias‑mitigation effect is achieved.

  • Why the other options are less suitable

    • A. Modify Solution1 to randomly generate emails for different traits – Introducing randomness does not address the underlying source of bias; it merely masks it and can produce incoherent or irrelevant content, reducing overall solution quality and stakeholder trust.
    • C. Retrain the model by using a larger dataset – A larger dataset may improve general performance but does not guarantee removal of bias. Moreover, retraining involves significant computational cost, data‑governance overhead, and may re‑introduce biases if the expanded data source is not carefully curated for fairness.
    • D. Modify the contents of the training dataset – Directly editing the training dataset can be effective, but it requires access to the original data, risk of altering model integrity, and extensive validation cycles. In a production‑deployed scenario, changing the dataset is often impractical and may violate model versioning policies.

Conclusion
Adjusting the system instructions offers a targeted, policy‑aligned, and operationally feasible method to mitigate bias while staying consistent with Microsoft’s Responsible AI framework.

References

  • Responsible AI principles for Azure OpenAI Service: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/concepts/responsible-ai
  • Guide to mitigating bias in Azure Machine Learning models: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-mitigate-bias

Note: The links above are active as of the knowledge cutoff date (2024‑06) and are the recommended Microsoft documentation sources for responsible AI and bias mitigation.

data.examQue:
A company has a canvas app named App1 in a Microsoft Power Platform environment named Env1.

Env1 uses a customer-managed key for data encryption. App1 connects to multiple data sources to retrieve and update customer and order information.

You need to recommend a solution to add Microsoft Copilot components to App1. The solution must NOT modify the current security or encryption configurations of Env1.

What should you include in the recommendation?
  • A. Modify the data sources of App1 to make them compatible with Copilot.
  • B. Duplicate App1 and republish the app in Env1.
  • C. Enable Copilot features for Env1.
  • D. Move App1 to a new environment that uses Microsoft-managed keys.
data.examAnsDesc:
Answer Overview
The only option that satisfies the requirement to add Copilot‑related functionality without altering the existing security posture (customer‑managed key) of Env1 is to move App1 to a new environment that uses Microsoft‑managed keys. This isolates the Copilot integration in a separate security context while preserving the original environment’s encryption configuration.

Why Option D Is Correct

  • Preserves encryption policy – The original environment (Env1) continues to use the customer‑managed key; only the duplicated workload runs in a new environment that uses Microsoft‑managed keys, leaving the source environment untouched.
  • Isolation of workloads – Copilot services may require additional permissions and network configurations that could conflict with customer‑owned encryption settings. Moving the app eliminates any need to adjust those settings in Env1.
  • Seamless Copilot enablement – Copilot is tightly integrated with Microsoft‑managed key environments; running it there guarantees compatibility and access to the full Copilot feature set.
  • Zero impact on existing production – The business can test and deploy Copilot enhancements in the new environment before any migration decision, ensuring no service interruption.

Why the Other Options Fail

  • A. Modify the data sources of App1 – Changing source connectors or schemas to force Compatibility with Copilot would require alterations to the security model (e.g., exposing new credentials) and could inadvertently affect the existing encryption scheme.
  • B. Duplicate App1 and republish in Env1 – Republishing in the same environment would still rely on the customer‑managed key, but Copilot components often need additional permissions and service endpoints that the current security framework does not support without re‑configuring encryption or network settings.
  • C. Enable Copilot features for Env1 – Directly enabling Copilot on an environment that enforces a customer‑managed key is not supported; Copilot requires Microsoft‑managed key encryption for its own data stores and may not function correctly under an external key hierarchy.

Conclusion – Because the goal is to integrate Copilot without touching the existing encryption configuration, the only viable approach is to relocate App1 to an environment that uses Microsoft‑managed keys, leaving the original environment unchanged.

References

  1. Microsoft Power Platform documentation – “Using customer‑managed keys with Power Apps” [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-platform/admin/customer-managed-keys]
  2. Microsoft Copilot for Power Apps – “Prerequisites and environment requirements” [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-copilot/requirements]

data.examQue:
Case study -

This is a case study. Case studies are not timed separately from other exam sections. You can use as much exam time as you would like to complete each case study. However, there might be additional case studies or other exam sections. Manage your time to ensure that you can complete all the exam sections in the time provided. Pay attention to the Exam Progress at the top of the screen so you have sufficient time to complete any exam sections that follow this case study.

To answer the case study questions, you will need to reference information that is provided in the case. Case studies and associated questions might contain exhibits or other resources that provide more information about the scenario described in the case. Information provided in an individual question does not apply to the other questions in the case study.

A Review Screen will appear at the end of this case study. From the Review Screen, you can review and change your answers before you move to the next exam section. After you leave this case study, you will NOT be able to return to it.


To start the case study -

To display the first question in this case study, select the "Next" button. To the left of the question, a menu provides links to information such as business requirements, the existing environment, and problem statements. Please read through all this information before answering any questions. When you are ready to answer a question, select the "Question" button to return to the question.


Overview -

Contoso, Ltd. is a high-tech manufacturing company that uses Microsoft Dynamics 365 Finance. Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management, and Dynamics 365 Commerce for its North American operations. The company designs and develops innovative products that have many patents and proprietary technologies. The patents and engineering designs are closely guarded secrets.

Contoso executives want to integrate and adopt AI solutions to help scale the company in preparation for an anticipated period of rapid growth.

The company has multiple legal entities and Azure subscriptions that will be used in the adopted AI solutions.


Requirements -


AI Adoption -

The following executives will have specific responsibilities in the overall AI adoption:

•Chief Technology Officer (CTO): Select one Dynamics 365 Finance, Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management or Dynamics 365 Commerce prebuilt AI agent and one custom Microsoft Copilot Studio AI agent to prioritize and deploy during the initial AI adoption phase.
•Chief Information Officer (CIO): Ensure that appropriate security labels are assigned to the data used by the AI agents.
•Chief Financial Officer (CFO): Analyze the return on investment (ROI) for the AI agents being deployed.
•Chief Information Security Officer (CISO): Discover and inventory AI resources for auditing.
•Chief Executive Officer (CEO): Ensure that all solutions adhere to industry-standard responsible AI practices.

All AI initiatives and agents will have a detailed business use case, a defined audience profile, and an estimated ROI that will compare the cost savings of the current process against the estimated costs of using the new AI solutions.

The company's research and development (R&D) department already has a custom Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that contains comprehensive product specifications and compliance data.


Prebuilt AI Agent -

The CTO has NOT yet selected which prebuilt AI agent to use in Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management. The CTO wants to view available agent templates to identify which agent will add the most business value.

Depending on which high-priority AI agents are identified, its agent capabilities must be previewed in a discovery meeting with the relevant business operation stakeholders.


Custom AI Agent -

Contoso has identified the following custom AI agent requirements:
•The custom AI agent will use data from Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management to answer questions for the manufacturing team as a low-code solution.
•The custom AI agent will be accessible from within Microsoft Teams.
•The custom AI agent must be designed to eventually connect to other agents that can be selected based on their description.
•The topics used in the custom AI agent will be selected based NOT on a trigger phrase, but on a description of the purpose of the query, to make the interactions more conversational.
•The custom AI agent must be able to answer questions about product specifications by using existing technologies. The product specifications are maintained by the R&D department.
•The custom AI agent must be integrated with and accessible from Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management.
•The custom AI agent must be able to use Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management business logic that is stored outside of the application.

Analysis, Reporting, and Troubleshooting

Contoso has identified the following analysis, reporting, and troubleshooting requirements:
•The CISO will audit all the AI solutions monthly for compliance and security.
•The CFO will analyze all the AI solutions quarterly to compare the estimated ROI against actual measured efficiencies and adoption. The CFO will use the Copilot Studio agent usage estimator to perform this analysis.
•The CISO wants to identify how much sensitive data was accessed for a given AI agent run and who accessed the data. Too much sensitive data accessed by a single user might indicate a high security risk.
•The CTO wants to track user feedback on the quality of the AI agent responses during user interactions with the agents. Consistently poor feedback will trigger an escalated reengineering discussion.
•The CEO wants a quarterly assessment of all the required metrics for their specific responsibilities. The tools used for the assessments must be Microsoft-recommended and must verify reliability, interpretability, fairness, and compliance.
•The CFO wants to identify how many interactions with the AI agents are abandoned on a given day as compared to resolved conversations. Too many abandoned sessions might indicate that Copilot Studio credits are being used inefficiently by end users.


Which two components for the custom AI agent should you include in the application lifecycle management (AIM) process? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.

NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
  • A. an Azure package
  • B. a ZIP package
  • C. a Microsoft Power Platform solution
  • D. a Cloud Scale Unit (CSU) package
  • E. an X++model
data.examAnsDesc:
Why options B and C are the correct components for the ALM process

  • B – a ZIP package – The custom Copilot Studio agent is exported as a compressed package that contains the conversational flows, authentication settings, and connections to back‑end services. This ZIP artifact is the binary deliverable that can be version‑controlled, reviewed, and promoted through the CI/CD pipelines recommended for Power Platform solutions.

  • C – a Microsoft Power Platform solution – The end‑to‑end lifecycle for a Copilot Studio agent is managed as a Power Platform solution (formerly known as a “pipeline‑enabled solution”). A solution bundles the ZIP content together with solution definitions, environment references, and dependency metadata, enabling automated deployment, source‑control integration, and rollback capabilities across dev, test, and prod environments.

Together, a ZIP package and a Power Platform solution constitute the actual deliverables that are packaged, tested, and moved through the ALM workflow.

Why the other options are not suitable

  • A – an Azure package – Azure packages are used to deploy Azure‑specific resources (e.g., ARM templates, Azure Functions). The Copilot Studio agent does not rely on a native Azure package for its lifecycle; its packaging is governed by the Power Platform framework instead.

  • D – a Cloud Scale Unit (CSU) package – CSU is a scaling mechanism for Azure Synapse or other large‑scale analytics services and has no relevance to the deployment or versioning of a conversational AI agent built in Copilot Studio.

  • E – an X++ model – X++ models belong to the Dynamics 365 Finance & Operations development stack and are used for extending the core platform. Custom Copilot Studio agents operate outside of the X++ development model and therefore do not involve X++ model artifacts in their ALM process.

References

  • Copilot Studio documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cognitive-services/copilot-studio/
  • Power Platform solution lifecycle: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-platform/alm/

These sources outline how agents are packaged as ZIP files and delivered as Power Platform solutions, supporting the ALM practices required for Contoso’s custom AI implementation.

What should you recommend to assist the CEO with their specific responsibilities?
  • A. the Microsoft Service Trust Portal
  • B. Microsoft Foundry Tools
  • C. Microsoft Purview
  • D. the Responsible AI dashboard
  • E. Compliance Center
data.examAnsDesc:
Why the correct option (D – the Responsible AI dashboard) is the best fit

  • The CEO’s mandate is to obtain a quarterly assessment of all required metrics that verifies reliability, interpretability, fairness, and compliance—exactly the set of capabilities exposed by the Responsible AI dashboard.
  • The dashboard aggregates model‑level metrics (accuracy, bias, drift), usage statistics, and audit logs, and it can be scheduled for regular reporting that aligns with the CEO’s quarterly cadence.
  • It is a Microsoft‑recommended, native tool for responsible AI governance, ensuring that the data and models used by the Dynamics 365 agents meet the company’s responsible‑AI policies without requiring third‑party solutions.

Why the other options are unsuitable

  • A. Microsoft Service Trust Portal – Provides visibility into Microsoft’s compliance certifications and security attestations, but it does not surface the granular AI‑specific performance and ethical metrics the CEO needs.
  • B. Microsoft Foundry Tools – Focuses on low‑code/Dataverse integration and automation pipelines; it lacks the AI‑centric risk and fairness analytics required for executive oversight.
  • C. Microsoft Purview – Excels at data cataloging, classification, and governance, yet it does not deliver the end‑to‑end AI model evaluation and responsible‑AI scoring that the CEO must report on.
  • E. Compliance Center – Centralizes compliance‑related activities (e.g., data loss prevention, audit logs) but is not tailored to AI model transparency, fairness analysis, or the generation of quarterly AI‑specific performance dashboards.

Therefore, the Responsible AI dashboard directly fulfills the CEO’s quarterly reporting requirements using Microsoft‑endorsed, AI‑focused metrics.

References

  • Responsible AI dashboard in Azure Machine Learning: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard
  • Overview of Microsoft’s Responsible AI principles and tools: https://learn.microsoft.com/responsible-ai/overview​
